blocks:
Ayahs

وَٱلذَّٰرِيَـٰتِ ذَرْوًا (١)
By the scattering [winds] that scatter [the clouds]; (1)
فَٱلْحَـٰمِلَـٰتِ وِقْرًا (٢)
by the [rain] bearing [clouds] laden [with water]; (2)
فَٱلْجَـٰرِيَـٰتِ يُسْرًا (٣)
by [the ships] which move gently [on the sea]; (3)
فَٱلْمُقَسِّمَـٰتِ أَمْرًا (٤)
by [the angels] who dispense [livelihood] by [His] command: (4)
إِنَّمَا تُوعَدُونَ لَصَادِقٌ (٥)
indeed what you are promised is true, (5)
وَإِنَّ ٱلدِّينَ لَوَٰقِعٌ (٦)
and indeed the retribution will surely come to pass! (6)

Content of Surah adh-Dhariyat

The primary axis of discussion in this Surah concerns matters related to the Hereafter, the Resurrection, and the reward and punishment of the believers and the criminals. However, in this regard, it is not exactly like "Surah Qaf"; rather, other focal points for discussion are also observed in this Surah.

Generally, it can be said that the topics of this Surah revolve around the following five axes:

1- As mentioned, a significant portion consists of discussions on the Resurrection and its various branches; the Surah both begins and ends with the theme of the Resurrection.

2- Another section of this Surah addresses the issue of Monotheism (Tawhid) and the signs and indicators of Allah in the system of creation, which naturally complements the discussions on the Resurrection.

3- In another part, it discusses the story of the angels who became guests of Ibrahim عليه السلام and were commissioned to demolish the cities of the people of Lut.

4- Other verses of this Surah contain brief references to the stories of Musa عليه السلام, the people of 'Ad, the people of Thamud, and the people of Nuh, thereby serving as a warning to other disbelievers and criminals.

5- Finally, another part of this Surah recounts the struggle of prejudiced and stubborn nations against past Prophets, thereby comforting the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, who faced adamant opponents, and inviting him to steadfastness.

The Virtue of Reciting This Surah

In a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, it is stated:

من قرأ سورة الذاريات فى يومه او ليلته اصلح اللَّه له معيشته و اتاه برزق واسع و نور له فى قبره بسراج يزهر الى يوم القيامة:

"Whoever recites Surah adh-Dhariyat during his day or night, Allah will rectify his life and livelihood, grant him an expansive provision, and illuminate his grave with a lamp that shines until the Day of Resurrection!" [1] We have repeatedly stated that mere lip service is not sufficient to attain such vast rewards; rather, the goal is a recitation that provokes thought, and thought that generates action! Additionally, the naming of the Surah as "adh-Dhariyat" is due to its correspondence with the first verse of this Surah.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, beginning of Surah adh-Dhariyat; Thawab al-A'mal as quoted in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 120.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ‌

وَ الذَّارِياتِ ذَرْواً (1) فَالْحامِلاتِ وِقْراً (2) فَالْجارِياتِ يُسْراً (3) فَالْمُقَسِّماتِ أَمْراً (4)

إِنَّما تُوعَدُونَ لَصادِقٌ (5) وَ إِنَّ الدِّينَ لَواقِعٌ (6)

Commentary

Oaths by the Storms and Rain-Bearing Clouds!

After Surah "as-Saffat," this is the second Surah that begins with repeated oaths—oaths full of meaning and thought-provoking, oaths that are awakening and informative.

Many other Surahs of the Quran, which God willing we will encounter in future discussions, are similar. Interestingly, these oaths are often a prelude to stating the issue of the Resurrection, except in a few cases related to Monotheism or other matters. It is also noteworthy that the content of these oaths has a specific connection to the reality of the Resurrection, pursuing this important topic from various angles with a particular subtlety and beauty.

The truth is that the oaths of the Quran, which are numerous, are one of the facets of the miracle of this Heavenly Book and among its most beautiful and brilliant passages, the explanation of each of which will come in its proper place.

At the beginning of this Surah, Allah has sworn by five different subjects, four of which appear consecutively and one part separately.

First, He says: "By the winds that move the clouds in the sky and scatter dust, plant seeds, and the pollen of flowers in every direction upon the earth" (وَ الذَّارِياتِ ذَرْواً) [1]

[^1]: "Dhariyat" is the plural of "Dhariyah," meaning winds that cause objects to fly/scatter.

Then He adds: "By the clouds that carry a heavy burden of rain" (فَالْحامِلاتِ وِقْراً) [1]

"And by the ships that move with ease upon the great rivers and the surface of the seas" (فَالْجارِياتِ يُسْراً) [2]

"And by the angels who distribute the affairs" (فَالْمُقَسِّماتِ أَمْراً). In a Hadith that many commentators have cited under this very verse, we read:

"Ibn al-Kawwa" [3] one day asked Ali عليه السلام while he was delivering a sermon from the pulpit: "What is meant by 'adh-Dhariyati dharwa'?" He replied: "The winds."

He asked: "And 'fal-hamilati wiqra'?" He replied: "The clouds."

He asked: "And 'fal-jariyati yusra'?" He replied: "The ships."

He asked: "And 'fal-muqassimati amra'?" He replied: "The angels."

Nevertheless, there are other interpretations that can be reconciled with this one. Among them is that "jariyati yusra" refers to the rivers that flow due to the rains, and "fal-muqassimati amra" refers to the provisions distributed through agriculture by means of the angels.

In this way, the talk is of winds, then clouds, then rivers, and finally the growth of plants, which has a close affinity with the issue of the Resurrection that follows. For we know that one of the proofs for the possibility of the Resurrection is the matter of reviving dead lands through the descent of rain, which has been mentioned many times in the Quran with various expressions.

The possibility has also been raised that these four descriptions all describe the winds: winds that produce clouds, winds that carry them on their backs, winds that drive them in every direction, and winds that scatter raindrops everywhere. [1] Given that the expressions in these verses are comprehensive and general, they can encompass all these meanings, but the primary interpretation is the first one.

Here the question arises: if the meaning is the angels, what affairs do the angels distribute?

In response, we say: this division of labor may relate to the overall management of this world, which groups of Divine angels are responsible for managing by His command. It may also relate to the distribution of provisions, or the distribution of raindrops over various regions of the earth. [2]

[^1]: "Wiqr" (rhyming with 'fikr') means a heavy load; it also means heaviness of the ear. "Waqar" also means gravity of movement, tranquility, and forbearance. [^2]: "Jariyat" is the plural of "Jariyah," meaning ship; it also means flowing rivers (فِيها عَيْنٌ جارِيَةٌ - Ghashiyah: 12), as well as the sun due to its movement in the sky. A young girl is also called "Jariyah" because the vitality of youth flows through her entire being. [^3]: His name was "Abdullah." He lived during the time of Amir al-Mu'minin Ali عليه السلام and was among the hypocrites and his fierce enemies; he would place himself in the ranks of his companions while engaging in sabotage.

[^1]: "Tafsir Fakhr al-Razi," Vol. 28, p. 195. [^2]: It is also necessary to note that the "waw" in "wa-dh-dhariyat" is the waw of oath, and the "fa" in the subsequent verses is the conjunctive "fa," which here carries the sense of the oath, while simultaneously expressing a type of connection and link between these four oaths.

After mentioning these four oaths, all of which express the importance of the matter that follows, He says: "That which you are promised is surely true" (إِنَّما تُوعَدُونَ لَصادِقٌ‌) [1]

And once more, as an emphasis, He adds: "And undoubtedly the recompense for deeds is bound to happen" (وَ إِنَّ الدِّينَ لَواقِعٌ‌). "Din" here means recompense (jazaa'), just as it appears in "Maliki yawmi-d-din" (Master of the Day of Recompense). Fundamentally, one of the names of the Resurrection is "Yawm al-Din" (the Day of Recompense). From this, it becomes clear that the "promises bound to happen" here refer to the promises regarding the Resurrection, the reckoning, reward and punishment, Paradise and Hell, and other matters related to the Hereafter. Therefore, the first sentence includes all the promises of the Resurrection, and the second sentence is an emphasis on the issue of recompense.

In a few verses later, "Yawm al-Din" is mentioned again. As we previously noted, the oaths at the beginning of this Surah have a clear link and affinity with the result of these oaths, because the movement of clouds, the descent of rain, and the subsequent revival of dead lands themselves demonstrate a scene of the Resurrection and the Hereafter in this world.

Some commentators have interpreted "ma tu'adun" (what you are promised) here in a broader sense, encompassing all Divine promises related to the Resurrection, the world, the distribution of provisions, the punishment of criminals in this world and the next, and the victory of the righteous believers. Verse 22 of this same Surah, which says: "And in the heaven is your provision and whatever you are promised" (وَ فِي السَّماءِ رِزْقُكُمْ وَ ما تُوعَدُونَ‌), may also support this meaning. Since the wording of the verse is absolute, this generality is not unlikely.

In any case, the Divine promises are all true, because "breaking a promise" stems either from "ignorance" or "incapacity"—ignorance that causes a change in the mind of the promisor, or incapacity that prevents him from fulfilling the promise. However, Allah is "All-Knowing" and "All-Powerful"; His promises are infallible.

[^1]: It should be noted that "ma" in "innama" is a relative pronoun (mawsulah) and the noun of "inna," and "sadiq" is its predicate (khabar).

Ayahs

وَٱلسَّمَآءِ ذَاتِ ٱلْحُبُكِ (٧)
By the sky full of adornment [with stars], (7)
إِنَّكُمْ لَفِى قَوْلٍ مُّخْتَلِفٍ (٨)
indeed you are of different opinions! (8)
يُؤْفَكُ عَنْهُ مَنْ أُفِكَ (٩)
He who has been turned away [from the truth] is turned away from it. (9)
قُتِلَ ٱلْخَرَّٰصُونَ (١٠)
Perish the liars, (10)
ٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ فِى غَمْرَةٍ سَاهُونَ (١١)
who are heedless in a stupor! (11)
يَسْـَٔلُونَ أَيَّانَ يَوْمُ ٱلدِّينِ (١٢)
They ask, ‘When will be the Day of Retribution?’ (12)
يَوْمَ هُمْ عَلَى ٱلنَّارِ يُفْتَنُونَ (١٣)
It is the day when they will be tormented in the Fire, (13)
ذُوقُوا۟ فِتْنَتَكُمْ هَـٰذَا ٱلَّذِى كُنتُم بِهِۦ تَسْتَعْجِلُونَ (١٤)
[and will be told]: ‘Taste your torment. This is what you used to hasten.’ (14)

Commentary

Swear by the Heaven and Its Beautiful Folds!

These verses, like the preceding ones, begin with an oath and speak of the disagreements among the disbelievers regarding the Resurrection and the Day of Judgment, as well as various other issues including the person of the Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family) and the matter of Monotheism (Tawhid).

First, He says: "By the heaven full of beautiful paths and folds" (وَ السَّماءِ ذاتِ الْحُبُكِ).

For the word hubuk (plural of hibak), linguists have provided many meanings, including: the paths and ripples formed by winds on desert sands, or on the surface of water, or in the clouds of the sky.

Curly or "wavy" hair is also referred to as hubuk.

Sometimes hubuk has been interpreted to mean beauty and adornment.

It also carries the meaning of a balanced and orderly form.

Its original root, habk, means to bind and make firm. [^1] It appears that all these meanings return to a single concept: the beautiful ripples and folds found among waves, clouds, desert sands, and the hair of the head.

As for applying this meaning to the sky, it is either because of the various shapes of star clusters and constellations (groups of fixed stars that take on a specific shape are called constellations), or because of the striking waves found in atmospheric clouds, which are sometimes so beautiful they capture human attention for a long time.

Or it refers to the massive clusters of galaxies that appear like the twists and turns of curly hair across the expanse of the sky; specifically, the remarkable photographs scientists have taken of these galaxies using telescopes vividly evoke the image of intricate, curly hair.

According to this meaning, the Quran swears by the heaven and these great galaxies, which at that time had not yet been seen by the keen eye of human science.

Given that these meanings are not contradictory, it is possible that all of them are intended in this oath. In verse 17 of Surah Al-Mu'minun, we also read: "And We have created above you seven paths" (وَ لَقَدْ خَلَقْنا فَوْقَكُمْ سَبْعَ طَرائِقَ), which refers to the diversity and multiplicity of the heavens, planets, galaxies, and different worlds. [^1] It is also noteworthy that the original root of hubuk could refer to the firmness of the heavens and the strong bond between celestial bodies, such as the planets of the solar system with the solar disk.

The next verse addresses the response to the oath—the point for which the oath was taken—and adds: "Indeed, you are in differing speech" (إِنَّكُمْ لَفِي قَوْلٍ مُخْتَلِفٍ).

[^1]: Refer to Lisan al-Arab and Raghib's Mufradat under the root h-b-k. [^1]: A detailed explanation of this verse is provided in Volume 14 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 219 onwards.

You constantly speak contradictions, and this very inconsistency is proof of the baselessness of your words.

Regarding the Resurrection, sometimes you say: "We do not believe at all that decayed bones can be brought back to life."

At other times you say: "We are in doubt and hesitation about this."

And sometimes you add: "Bring back our fathers and ancestors to testify that there is a Resurrection after death so that we may believe!"

Regarding the Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family), sometimes you say he is mad, sometimes you call him a poet, sometimes you label him a sorcerer, and sometimes you say he has a teacher who instructs him in these words.

Similarly, regarding the Quran, sometimes you call it "fables of the ancients" (myths and superstitions of the past), sometimes you call it poetry, sometimes magic, and sometimes a lie! By the folds of the heavens, your words are full of contradictions and twists! If you had any foundation or substance, you would at least stand by one claim and not pursue a different story every day.

This expression is, in fact, an argument for the invalidity of the opponents' claims regarding Monotheism, the Resurrection, the Prophet, and the Quran (although the primary focus of these verses, as evidenced by the verses that follow, is on the issue of the Resurrection).

We know that to uncover the lies of false claimants, whether in judicial matters or otherwise, their contradictory statements are always cited; the Quran relies precisely on this point.

In the following verse, He explains the cause of this deviation from the truth, saying: "Turned away from it [faith in the Resurrection] is he who is averted [from accepting the proofs of truth and submitting to logic]," otherwise, the proofs of life after death are manifest (يُؤْفَكُ عَنْهُ مَنْ أُفِكَ).

It should be noted that the expression in the verse is general and concise, the literal translation of which would be: "Averted from it are those who are averted."

This is because ifk originally means to turn away or divert from something; hence, a "lie" is called ifk because of its deviating nature, just as shifting winds are called mu'tafikat.

However, since the preceding verses discussed the Resurrection, it is clear that the primary meaning is deviation from this belief. Furthermore, since the previous verse spoke of the contradictory talk of the disbelievers, it becomes clear that the reference here is to those who deviate from clear logic and evidence.

Therefore, the overall meaning of the verse is: those who are diverted from faith in the Resurrection are those who have deviated from the path of rational evidence and the logic of seeking the truth.

Of course, there is no obstacle to interpreting it as a deviation from accepting any truth, including the Quran, Monotheism, the Prophethood of the Prophet, and the Resurrection (including the issue of the Wilayah of the Infallible Imams, as mentioned in some narrations); however, in any case, the issue of the Resurrection, which is the main subject, is certainly included.

In the next verse, He severely censures and threatens the liars and fabricators, saying: "Cursed be the liars [and death upon them]!" (قُتِلَ الْخَرَّاصُونَ).

Kharras is derived from khars, which originally means any speech uttered based on conjecture and guesswork. Since such speech often turns out to be false, this word is also used to mean "lying." Thus, the kharrassun are those who speak baseless and nonsensical words, and here—by the context of the following verses—it refers to those who judge the Resurrection with baseless words devoid of logic.

In any case, this sentence is in the form of a curse upon them—a curse that shows they are beings deserving of death and destruction, such that their "non-existence is better than their existence."

Some have also interpreted qutila here to mean being cursed, rejected, and deprived of Allah's mercy.

From this, one can also derive a general rule: judgments that lack clear evidence and are based on unfounded guesses, conjectures, and suspicions are misleading acts deserving of curse and punishment.

Then, He proceeds to identify these lying kharrassun, adding: "Those who are in a flood [of ignorance], heedless" (الَّذِينَ هُمْ فِي غَمْرَةٍ ساهُونَ).

Ghamrah originally means abundant water that covers a place; subsequently, it was applied to deep ignorance and unawareness that envelops a person.

Sahun is derived from sahw, meaning any kind of heedlessness. Some have said that the first stage of ignorance is sahw (oversight/mistake), then ghaflah (heedlessness), and after that is ghamrah.

Thus, they begin from the stage of oversight, which leads to heedlessness and unawareness, and as they continue the path, they become completely submerged in "ignorance." The combination of these two terms—sahw and ghamrah—in the above verse may refer to the beginning and the end of this process.

Accordingly, the kharrassun are those who are drowned in their own ignorance and, to escape the burden of truth, bring up a new excuse or baseless statement every day.

Therefore, "They ask, 'When is the Day of Recompense?' [and when will the Resurrection come?]" (يَسْئَلُونَ أَيَّانَ يَوْمُ الدِّينِ).

The use of yas'alun in the "present/future tense" indicates that they raise this question repeatedly, whereas the timing of the Resurrection must fundamentally remain hidden and concealed, so that everyone at any time considers the possibility of its occurrence, and the educational effect of faith in the Resurrection—which is self-improvement and constant readiness—is achieved.

This is like a patient repeatedly asking a doctor, "On what day will my life end?" Anyone would consider this question baseless and would say: the important thing is for you to know that death is a reality so that you treat yourself, lest you fall victim to a "premature death."

However, they had no goal other than mockery or excuse-seeking; it was not that they truly wanted to find the date of the Resurrection.

Nevertheless, the Quran gives them a crushing response, saying: "[The Resurrection is] the Day they will be refined [burned] over the Fire!" (يَوْمَ هُمْ عَلَى النَّارِ يُفْتَنُونَ).

And it will be said to them: "Taste your trial [punishment]! This is what you were seeking to hasten" (ذُوقُوا فِتْنَتَكُمْ هذَا الَّذِي كُنْتُمْ بِهِ تَسْتَعْجِلُونَ).

Fitnah originally means placing gold in a furnace to distinguish good, pure gold from the impure. For this reason, it is used to mean any kind of trial or examination. It has also been used to mean "the entry of a human into fire," and sometimes to mean affliction, punishment, and distress, as the verse under discussion refers to this very meaning.

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ فِى جَنَّـٰتٍ وَعُيُونٍ (١٥)
Indeed the Godwary will be amid gardens and springs, (15)
ءَاخِذِينَ مَآ ءَاتَىٰهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ ۚ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ قَبْلَ ذَٰلِكَ مُحْسِنِينَ (١٦)
receiving what their Lord has given them, for they had been virtuous aforetime. (16)
كَانُوا۟ قَلِيلًا مِّنَ ٱلَّيْلِ مَا يَهْجَعُونَ (١٧)
They used to sleep a little during the night, (17)
وَبِٱلْأَسْحَارِ هُمْ يَسْتَغْفِرُونَ (١٨)
and at dawns they would plead for forgiveness, (18)
وَفِىٓ أَمْوَٰلِهِمْ حَقٌّ لِّلسَّآئِلِ وَٱلْمَحْرُومِ (١٩)
and there was a share in their wealth for the beggar and the deprived. (19)

Commentary

The Reward of the Righteous Who Rise at Night

Following the previous verses which spoke of the ignorant liars, the deniers of the Resurrection, and their punishment, the verses under discussion speak of the God-fearing believers, their attributes, and their rewards, so that through comparison—as is the method of the Quran—realities become clearer.

It says: "Indeed, the righteous will be among gardens and springs" (إِنَّ الْمُتَّقِينَ فِي جَنَّاتٍ وَ عُيُونٍ‌).

It is true that a garden naturally possesses streams of water, but the beauty lies in the springs gushing from within the garden itself, constantly watering the trees. This is a distinction held by the gardens of Paradise; not just one type of spring, but various types are present therein. [^1]

Then, referring to other heavenly blessings, it states in a general manner: "Receiving what their Lord has given them" (آخِذِينَ ما آتاهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ‌).

This means they accept these Divine gifts with utmost desire, eagerness, complete satisfaction, and joy.

The verse continues by adding that these great rewards are not without reason: "Indeed, they were before that doers of good in the worldly life" (إِنَّهُمْ كانُوا قَبْلَ ذلِكَ مُحْسِنِينَ‌). [^2]

[^1]: The word "in" (فِي) regarding "gardens" (جَنَّات) has a clear meaning, as the righteous are inside Paradise. However, regarding "springs" (عُيُون), it does not mean they are inside the springs themselves, but rather they are situated amidst the flowing springs.

[^2]: The phrase "before that" (قَبْلَ ذلِكَ‌), as mentioned, refers to the time before the Day of Judgment and entry into Paradise—meaning in the world. However, some have interpreted it to mean before the arrival of the Divine Law, suggesting they acted upon "intellectual constants" even before the revelation; but this interpretation seems far-fetched.

The term "Ihsan" (doing good) mentioned here has a broad meaning that encompasses both obedience to Allah and various types of kindness toward Allah's creation.

The subsequent verses elaborate on how they were "doers of good," describing three of their attributes. First: "They used to sleep but little of the night" (كانُوا قَلِيلًا مِنَ اللَّيْلِ ما يَهْجَعُونَ‌).

"Yahja'un" (يَهْجَعُونَ) is derived from "Huju'" (هجوع), meaning nocturnal sleep.

Some have said this means they were awake for most of the night and slept only a little, essentially being constant night-vigilants.

However, since this seems unlikely as a general command for all the righteous and doers of good, this interpretation is not suitable. Rather, it means it was rare for them to sleep through the entire night. In other words, "night" (لَيْل) is considered as a genus or a general category.

Therefore, every night they were awake for a portion of it, engaging in worship and the Night Prayer (Salat al-Layl), and the nights in which they slept entirely and completely missed the nightly worship were few.

This interpretation has also been narrated in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام. Other interpretations have been mentioned for this verse, but because they were far-fetched, they were omitted. [^1]

[^1]: The late Tabari in Majma' al-Bayan referred to this Hadith (Vol. 9, p. 155). In Tafsir al-Safi, this Hadith is also narrated from al-Kafi as follows: "There were very few nights that passed them by in which they did not stand [in prayer]" (Tafsir al-Safi under the verse).

The second attribute is described as: "And in the hours before dawn they would ask for forgiveness" (وَ بِالْأَسْحارِ هُمْ يَسْتَغْفِرُونَ‌). At the end of the night, when the eyes of the heedless are asleep, the environment is calm from every perspective, the clamor of material life has subsided, and the factors that occupy human thought are silent, they rise and go to the threshold of Allah. They engage in intimate prayer (Munajat) before the Beloved, perform prayers, and specifically seek forgiveness for their sins.

Many believe that "seeking forgiveness" (Istighfar) here refers to the "Night Prayer" itself, because the "Qunut of the Witr prayer" includes seeking forgiveness.

"Ashar" (أَسْحار) is the plural of "Sahar" (سحر), which originally means "to be covered or hidden." Because a specific concealment prevails over everything in the final hours of the night, it is called "Sahar."

The word "Sihr" (magic) is also applied to something that covers the face of reality or whose secrets are hidden from others.

In a narration in Tafsir al-Durr al-Manthur, it is stated that the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله said:

"The end of the night for Tahajjud (Night Prayer) is more beloved to me than its beginning, for Allah says: 'And in the hours before dawn they would ask for forgiveness.'" [^1] And in another Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read:

"The righteous of Paradise used to seek forgiveness from Allah in the Witr prayer at dawn seventy times." [^2]

Then, referring to the third attribute of the righteous of Paradise, it adds: "And in their wealth was a right for the petitioner and the deprived" (وَ فِي أَمْوالِهِمْ حَقٌّ لِلسَّائِلِ وَ الْمَحْرُومِ‌). The use of the word "right" (حَقّ) here is either because Allah has made it obligatory upon them (like Zakat, Khums, and other mandatory religious dues) or they have made it obligatory upon themselves and committed to it; in this case, it also includes non-obligatory rights.

Some believe this verse only refers to the second type and does not include obligatory rights, because obligatory rights exist in the wealth of all people, whether righteous or otherwise, and even disbelievers. Therefore, when it says "in their wealth is such a right," it means that in addition to the obligations, they consider it necessary upon themselves to spend from their wealth in the way of Allah for the petitioners and the deprived. However, it can be said that the difference between the doers of good and others is that they fulfill these rights, while others are not committed to them.

It has also been said that the term "petitioner" (سائل) refers to obligatory rights, as he has the right to ask and demand, while the term "deprived" (محروم) refers to recommended rights where there is no right to demand.

Fadil Miqdad in Kanz al-'Irfan explicitly states that "known right" (حق معلوم) is a right they themselves place in their wealth and consider themselves duty-bound to fulfill. [^1] A similar meaning appears in Surah al-Ma'arij, verses 24 and 25: "And those in whose wealth is a known right, for the petitioner and the deprived."

Given that the ruling on the obligation of Zakat was revealed in Medina, and the verses of this Surah are all Meccan, the latter view is supported.

In narrations from the sources of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام, it is also emphasized that "known right" refers to something other than the obligatory Zakat.

In a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "But Allah, the Almighty and Majestic, has ordained rights in the wealth of the rich other than Zakat. He said: 'And those in whose wealth is a known right for the petitioner...' Thus, the known right is other than Zakat; it is something a man imposes upon himself in his wealth... if he wishes, every day, or if he wishes, every Friday, or if he wishes, every month." [^2] In this regard, several other Hadiths with different expressions have been narrated from Imam Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه السلام, Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, and Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام. [^3] Thus, the interpretation of the verse is clear.

Regarding the difference between the "petitioner" (سائل) and the "deprived" (محروم), a group has said: The "petitioner" is one who asks people for help, but the "deprived" is a dignified person who exerts the utmost effort for his livelihood but achieves nothing, and his business and life are entangled; yet, he maintains self-restraint and does not ask anyone for help.

This is the same person referred to as "Muharaf" (محارف), for in the interpretation of Muharaf in lexicons and Islamic narrations, it is said: "He is one who, no matter how hard he tries, earns no income, as if the paths of life are closed to him."

In any case, this expression points to the fact that you should never sit and wait for the needy to come to you and ask for help. It is upon you to search and find the dignified deprived individuals whom the Quran describes (Baqarah: 273): "The ignorant one thinks them wealthy because of their restraint." You should find them, help them, untie the knots of their problems, and preserve their dignity. This is an important instruction that is vital for maintaining the prestige of deprived Muslims.

Of course, these individuals (as the Quran says in the same verse of Baqarah) can be recognized by their faces: "You will know them by their mark."

Yes, though they are silent, in the depths of their faces, the signs of agonizing internal suffering are apparent to the aware, and "the color of their face reveals their inner secret."

Key Points

1. Attention to "Allah" and "Allah's Creation"

The attributes mentioned for the "righteous" (Muttaqin) and "doers of good" (Muhsinin) in these verses are essentially summarized in two parts: attention to the Creator, specifically during hours when one is fully prepared for intimate prayer with Him and heart-presence is achieved, and factors of mental distraction are at a minimum—meaning late at night.

And secondly, attention to the needs of the needy, whether they manifest their need or keep it hidden.

This is the same point repeatedly recommended in the verses of the Quran. The verses that list "prayer" (Salat) and "charity" (Zakat) side by side and emphasize both refer to this very issue, because "prayer" is the most prominent manifestation of the bond with the Creator, and "charity" is the clearest way of bonding with Allah's creation.

2. Rise at Night, for Lovers Reveal Secrets at Night!

Although the Night Prayer is among the supererogatory and recommended prayers, it is repeatedly mentioned in the Holy Quran, which indicates its extraordinary importance. The Quran considers it a means to reach the "Praised Station" (Maqam Mahmud) (Surah al-Isra: 79) and a "source of delight for the eyes" (as stated in verse 17 of Surah al-Sajdah).

In Islamic narrations, extraordinary emphasis is placed on this nightly intimate prayer and wakefulness at dawn:

In one place, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله considers it an expiation for sins and says: "O Ali, there are three expiations, one of which is Tahajjud at night while the people are asleep." [^1] In another Hadith from the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, it is said: "The nobles of my Ummah are the carriers of the Quran and the companions of the night [worship]." [^2] Again, in another Hadith regarding his advice to Ali عليه السلام, it is mentioned that he repeated four times: "You must perform the Night Prayer." [^3] And from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, in the interpretation of the verse under discussion (They used to sleep but little of the night), it is narrated: "Few were the nights that passed them by in which they did not wake up and worship." [^4] In another Hadith, it is stated that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "Two units of prayer in the heart of the night are more beloved to me than the world and all that is in it!" [^5] We also read in a Hadith that Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام said to Sulayman al-Daylami (one of his companions): "Do not abandon the night vigil, for the one who is cheated (Maghbun) is he who is deprived of the night vigil." [^1] Of course, narrations in this field are many, and exceptionally beautiful expressions are found in them. Specifically, the Night Prayer is introduced as an effective means for the forgiveness of sins, the awakening of thought and reflection, the illumination of the heart, the attraction of abundant sustenance, and physical health. If collected, they would form a separate book. [^2]

3. The Right of the Petitioner and the Deprived

It is noteworthy that in the above verses, we read that there is always a "right" in the wealth of the doers of good for the petitioner and the deprived. This expression clearly shows that they see themselves as indebted to the needy and deprived, and consider them as creditors and right-holders—a right that must be paid in any case, and there is no sense of favor-granting in paying it, just like the debts of other creditors.

As we have said, various clues show that this expression does not pertain to the obligatory Zakat and the like, but rather refers to recommended spending which the righteous consider as their debt. [^3]

Ayahs

وَفِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ءَايَـٰتٌ لِّلْمُوقِنِينَ (٢٠)
In the earth are signs for those who have conviction, (20)
وَفِىٓ أَنفُسِكُمْ ۚ أَفَلَا تُبْصِرُونَ (٢١)
and in your souls [as well]. Will you not then perceive? (21)
وَفِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ رِزْقُكُمْ وَمَا تُوعَدُونَ (٢٢)
And in the sky is your provision and what you are promised. (22)
فَوَرَبِّ ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ إِنَّهُۥ لَحَقٌّ مِّثْلَ مَآ أَنَّكُمْ تَنطِقُونَ (٢٣)
By the Lord of the sky and the earth, it is indeed the truth, just as [it is a fact that] you speak. (23)

Commentary

Signs of Allah are within your own being; do you not see?

Following the previous verses which spoke about the issue of the Resurrection and the characteristics of the people of Hell and Paradise, the verses under discussion speak of the signs of Allah on the earth and within the human being himself. This is so that, on one hand, they may become acquainted with the issue of Monotheism (Tawhid) and the recognition of Allah and His attributes—which is the starting point of movement toward all goodness—and on the other hand, with His power over the Resurrection and life after death; for the Creator of life on earth and all these wonders and marvels is also capable of restoring life.

First, He says: "And on the earth are signs for those who have certainty and are seekers of truth" (وَ فِي الْأَرْضِ آياتٌ لِلْمُوقِنِينَ).

Truly, the signs of the Truth, His infinite power, and His boundless knowledge and wisdom on this very earthly sphere are so abundant that no human lifespan is sufficient to recognize them all.

The volume of the earth, its distance from the sun, its rotation around itself, its revolution around the sun, and the forces of gravity and repulsion that arise from that volume and this movement—which are perfectly balanced and equivalent to one another—and the harmony of all these together to provide the conditions for life on the surface of the earth, are all among the great signs of Allah.

Indeed, if even the slightest change were to occur in one of these movements, relationships, or characteristics, the conditions for life on the surface of the earth would be disrupted, and the balances would be shattered.

The materials that compose the earth, and the various resources prepared on the surface and underground for life and living, are each a sign among His signs.

The mountains and plains, valleys and forests, rivers and springs—each of which plays an effective role in sustaining life and harmonizing its conditions—are other signs.

Hundreds of thousands of species of plants, insects, and animals (yes, hundreds of thousands of species), each with its own characteristics and wonders that plunge a person into amazement when studying books of geology, botany, and zoology, are further signs.

In every corner of this earthly sphere, there are fascinating secrets that perhaps few people notice, but the inquisitive gaze of scientists has unveiled them and revealed the greatness of the Creator.

It is not out of place here to listen to a portion of the words of one of the world's famous scientists who has conducted extensive studies in this field:

A. Cressy Morrison says: "In the adjustment of natural factors, the utmost precision and detail have been employed. For example, if the earth's crust were ten feet thicker than it is, oxygen—the essential substance of life—would not exist. Or if the depth of the oceans were a few feet more than their current depth, then all the oxygen and carbon of the earth would be absorbed, and no possibility of any plant or animal life would remain on the surface of the soil."

Elsewhere, regarding the layer of air surrounding the earth, he says:

"If the air surrounding the earth were slightly thinner than it is, the meteors that are attracted toward the earth by the millions every day and are exploded and destroyed in the space outside the earth (due to collision with the air layer) would constantly reach the surface of the earth and strike every corner of it! Or if the speed of the meteors' movement were less than it is (they would never explode upon contact with the air), they would all fall to the surface of the earth, and the result of their destruction would be evident."

And elsewhere he says: "Only twenty-one percent of the air surrounding the earth is oxygen... If the amount of oxygen in the air were fifty percent instead of twenty-one percent, all the combustible materials in this world would burn, and if a spark reached a tree in a forest, the entire forest would burn completely!" The density of the air surrounding the earth is such that it allows cosmic rays to pass toward the earth to the extent necessary for the growth and development of plants, and it destroys all harmful microbes in that space and creates beneficial vitamins.

Or the existence of various vapors that have emerged from the depths of the earth over many centuries and dispersed into the air, most of which are toxic gases; nevertheless, the air surrounding the earth has not become polluted and has always remained in that balanced state suitable for the continuation of human life.

The vast mechanism that creates this wonderful balance and maintains the equilibrium is the sea and the ocean, from whose source of grace vital materials, food, rain, the moderation of the air, plants, and ultimately the existence of man himself originate.

Anyone who perceives meanings must bow in respect before the greatness of the sea and be grateful for its blessings (and the Creator of the sea). [1]

In the next verse, He adds: "And in your own selves [are signs of Allah's power]" (وَ فِي أَنْفُسِكُمْ). "Do you not then open your eyes and see all these clear signs of the Truth?" (أَ فَلا تُبْصِرُونَ).

Undoubtedly, the human being is the marvel of the universe, and whatever exists in the macrocosm also exists in this microcosm; rather, there are wonders in it that are found nowhere else in the world.

The wonder is that this human being, with such intelligence, reason, knowledge, and all this creativity, innovation, and marvelous industries, was on the first day in the form of a small and worthless drop of fluid (nutfah); but as soon as he is placed in the world of the womb, he moves toward perfection with extraordinary speed, changing shape day by day and transforming moment by moment, and that insignificant drop is converted into a complete human being in a short period.

A single cell, which is the smallest component of his body, has a multi-layered and wondrous structure that, according to scientists, contains the equivalent of an industrial city's facilities!

One biological scientist says: "This vast city with thousands of fascinating doors and gates, thousands of factories, warehouses, and piping networks, and a command center with its many facilities and numerous communications and various vital tasks—all within the small confines of a single cell—is among the most complex and amazing cities. If we wanted to build facilities that perform the same functions—and we are never able to—we would have to put tens of thousands of hectares of land under various facilities, buildings, and complex machinery to be ready for such a program. But the interesting thing is that the mechanism of creation has placed all of this in an area equivalent to fifteen-millionths of a millimeter!" [1] The systems within the human body, such as the heart, kidneys, lungs, and especially the tens of thousands of kilometers of large and small vessels and even capillaries that cannot be seen with the eye and are responsible for water delivery, nutrition, and ventilation for the "ten million billion" cells of the human body, and the various senses such as sight, hearing, and other senses, are each a great sign among His signs.

And most importantly, the enigma of "life," whose secrets remain unknown, and the structure of the human soul and intellect, which the intellects of all humans are incapable of comprehending. It is here that man involuntarily opens his lips in glorification, praise, and thanksgiving to Allah, bows his head in the presence of His greatness, and chants these verses:

In You, O marvel of the universe, thought has become weary and exhausted! You have bewildered the possessors of intellect and confused the minds. Whenever my thought advances a span toward You, it flees a mile, Turning back, stumbling in darkness, finding no way forward!

In a Hadith, it is narrated that the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله said: "Whoever knows himself has known his Lord." [1] Yes, "self-knowledge" is the path to "God-knowledge" at every stage.

The expression "أَ فَلا تُبْصِرُونَ" (Do you not see?) is a subtle expression, meaning that these divine signs are all around you, within your soul, and throughout your entire body; if you open your eyes a little, you will see, and your soul will be quenched by the perception of His greatness.

In the third verse under discussion, He points to the third category of the signs of the Lord's greatness and His power over the Resurrection, saying: "And in the heaven is your provision and that which you are promised" (وَ فِي السَّماءِ رِزْقُكُمْ وَ ما تُوعَدُونَ).

Although in some Islamic narrations "provision" (rizq) in this verse has been interpreted as the life-giving drops of rain, which are the source of every good and blessing on earth—and verse 5 of Surah Al-Jathiyah also agrees with this: "And what Allah has sent down from the heaven of provision and thereby given life to the earth after its death"—this meaning can be one of the clear instances of the verse. Meanwhile, the breadth of the concept of provision includes rain, the sunlight that comes to us from the sky and whose role in life is extremely sensitive, and also the air, which is the source of life for all living beings.

All of this is if we interpret "heaven" (sama') as this physical sky; however, some commentators have taken "heaven" to mean the world of the Unseen, the metaphysical realm, and the Preserved Tablet (Lawh al-Mahfuz), from where the destinies of human provisions are determined.

Of course, combining both meanings is possible, although the first interpretation seems clearer.

As for the phrase "ما تُوعَدُونَ" (that which you are promised), it could be an emphasis on the issue of provision and the divine promise in this regard, or it could mean the promised Paradise, as we read in verse 15 of Surah An-Najm: "Near it is the Garden of Abode" (meaning the promised Paradise is near the Lote Tree of the Utmost Boundary in the heavens). Or it could refer to any kind of good, blessing, or punishment that descends from the heaven, or it could encompass all these concepts, as the phrase "ما تُوعَدُونَ" is broad and extensive in meaning.

In any case, these three verses have a subtle order: the first verse speaks of the factors of human existence on the planet Earth, the second verse speaks of the human existence itself, and the third verse speaks of the factors of his endurance and survival.

This point is also noteworthy: that which prevents human insight and stops one from studying the secrets of creation, the secrets of the earth, and the wonders of one's own existence is greed for provision. In the last verse, Allah reassures man that his provision is guaranteed, so that he can look at the wonders of the universe with a peaceful mind, and the phrase "أَ فَلا تُبْصِرُونَ" may be realized in him.

Therefore, to emphasize this matter, He swears an oath in the final verse under discussion, saying: "Then by the Lord of the heaven and the earth, it is surely the truth, just as [it is true] that you speak" (فَوَ رَبِّ السَّماءِ وَ الْأَرْضِ إِنَّهُ لَحَقٌّ مِثْلَ ما أَنَّكُمْ تَنْطِقُونَ).

It has come to the point where Allah, with all His greatness and power, swears an oath to reassure skeptical, slow-to-believe, weak-willed, and greedy servants that what has been promised to you regarding provision, and the promises of reward and punishment in the Resurrection, is all the truth, and there is no doubt or hesitation in it. [1] The expression "مِثْلَ ما أَنَّكُمْ تَنْطِقُونَ" (just as you speak) is a subtle and calculated expression that speaks of the most perceptible things; for sometimes an error occurs in human sight or hearing, but in speaking, such an error does not occur where a person feels he has spoken when he has not. Therefore, the Quran says: just as your speaking is perceptible and real to you, the provision and divine promises are also like this! Furthermore, the matter of speaking itself is one of the greatest provisions and blessings of the Lord, which no living creature besides man enjoys, and the role of speaking in the social life of humans, education, the transmission of sciences and knowledge, and solving life's problems is hidden from no one.

Key Points

1- The Stirring Story of Asma'i

Zamakhshari in the commentary "Al-Kashshaf" narrates from Asma'i [1]: I came out of the mosque of Basra, and suddenly my eyes fell upon a desert Arab riding his mount. He met me and said: "From which tribe are you?" I said: "From Bani Asma'." He said: "Where are you coming from?" I said: "From where they recite the words of Allah, the Most Merciful." He said: "Recite for me as well!" I recited verses from Surah Adh-Dhariyat for him until I reached the verse "And in the heaven is your provision." He said: "That is enough." He stood up, slaughtered the camel he had with him, and distributed its meat among the needy who were coming and going. He also broke his sword and bow, threw them aside, turned his back, and left. This story passed.

When I went to visit the House of Allah with Harun al-Rashid, I was busy with circumambulation (Tawaf) when suddenly I heard someone calling me in a low voice. I looked and saw it was the same Arab man; he had become thin, and his face had turned yellow (it was evident that a burning love had overcome him and made him restless). He greeted me and requested that I recite the same Surah Adh-Dhariyat for him once more. When I reached the same verse, he let out a cry and said: "We have found the promise of our Lord to be true." Then he added: "Is there any verse after this?" I read the next verse: "Then by the Lord of the heaven and the earth, it is surely the truth." Once again he cried out and said: "Glory be to Allah! Who is it that has angered the Majestic One so that He had to swear an oath so they would believe His word, until they forced Him to take an oath?!" He repeated this sentence three times, fell to the ground, and the bird of his soul flew to the heavens. [1]

2- Where is Paradise?

As we said in the commentary of the verses, some have interpreted the phrase "وَ ما تُوعَدُونَ" to mean Paradise and have said it is inferred from this verse that Paradise is in the heavens. However, this statement does not seem consistent with what is mentioned in verse 133 of Surah Al-Imran, which says: "Paradise, whose width is as the heavens and the earth."

And as we said, this interpretation for the phrase "what you are promised" is not certain; rather, it may refer to the promise of provision or heavenly punishments.

And if it is mentioned in verse 15 of Surah An-Najm that "Jannat al-Ma'wa" is in the heaven near the "Lote Tree of the Utmost Boundary," it would not be proof for this meaning, because "Jannat al-Ma'wa" is a part of the gardens of Paradise, not the entire Paradise (take note).

3- Benefiting from the signs of the Truth requires readiness

When the verses of the Quran speak of the secrets of creation and the signs of Allah in the universe, sometimes it says: "These are signs for a people who listen" (لِقَوْمٍ يَسْمَعُونَ) (Yunus 67). And sometimes it says: "For a people who reflect" (لِقَوْمٍ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ) (Ra'd 3). Sometimes it says: "For a people who use reason" (لِقَوْمٍ يَعْقِلُونَ) (Ra'd 4). Sometimes it says: "For every patient and grateful one" (لِكُلِّ صَبَّارٍ شَكُورٍ) (Ibrahim 5). Sometimes it says: "For a people who believe" (لِقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ) (Nahl 79). Sometimes it says: "Signs for those of understanding" (لَآياتٍ لِأُولِي النُّهى) (Taha 54). Sometimes it says: "Signs for those who discern" (لَآياتٍ لِلْمُتَوَسِّمِينَ) (Hijr 75). And finally, sometimes it says: "Signs for those who know" (لَآياتٍ لِلْعالِمِينَ) (Rum 22).

And in the verses under discussion, it says: "Do you not see?" Meaning the signs of Allah on the earth and within your own being are clear for those who have seeing eyes.

These expressions clearly show that to use and benefit from the countless signs and many indicators that exist for His Pure Being throughout the universe, a prepared ground is necessary: a seeing eye, a listening ear, an awake mind, an alert heart, and a soul ready to accept and thirsty for truths are required. Otherwise, a person might live for years amidst these signs but, like animals, recognize nothing but the stable and the grass.

4- Provision is a Right

Among the matters governed by a precise system is this very issue of provision, to which clear references are made in the verses under discussion. It is true that effort and striving are conditions for benefiting from the blessings of life, and laziness and lethargy are causes of deprivation and helplessness; but it is also a mistake to imagine that with greed, avarice, and irregular actions, a person's provision will increase, or that with modesty, dignity, and self-restraint, provision will decrease.

Interesting expressions are seen in Islamic Hadiths in this regard. In a Hadith from the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: "Provision is not pulled by the greed of a greedy person, nor is it turned away by the dislike of one who dislikes it." [1] In another Hadith from Imam Sadiq عليه السلام, it is narrated that in response to someone who had requested an exhortation, he said: "And if provision is apportioned, then why the greed?..." [2]

The goal of these statements is not to stop effort, but rather to restrain greedy individuals from their greed by drawing attention to the fact that provision is predestined.

This point is also noteworthy: in Islamic Hadiths, many things are introduced as means of attracting provision or obstacles to it, each of which is constructive in its own turn. In a Hadith from Imam Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "By Him who sent my grandfather in truth as a Prophet, Allah, Blessed and Exalted be He, provides for the servant according to his chivalry and character, and help descends according to the severity of the trial." [1] In another Hadith from the same Imam, it is said: "Refraining from harming people and minimizing clamor increase provision." [2] It is also narrated from the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله that he said: "Monotheism is half of religion, and bring down provision through charity!" [3] And likewise, other matters such as cleaning the surroundings of the house and washing and cleaning vessels are introduced as causes for increasing provision.

Ayahs

هَلْ أَتَىٰكَ حَدِيثُ ضَيْفِ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ ٱلْمُكْرَمِينَ (٢٤)
Did you receive the story of Abraham’s honoured guests? (24)
إِذْ دَخَلُوا۟ عَلَيْهِ فَقَالُوا۟ سَلَـٰمًا ۖ قَالَ سَلَـٰمٌ قَوْمٌ مُّنكَرُونَ (٢٥)
When they entered into his presence, they said, ‘Peace!’ ‘Peace!’ He answered, ‘[You are] an unfamiliar folk.’ (25)
فَرَاغَ إِلَىٰٓ أَهْلِهِۦ فَجَآءَ بِعِجْلٍ سَمِينٍ (٢٦)
Then he retired to his family and brought a fat [roasted] calf, (26)
فَقَرَّبَهُۥٓ إِلَيْهِمْ قَالَ أَلَا تَأْكُلُونَ (٢٧)
and put it near them. He said, ‘Will you not eat?’ (27)
فَأَوْجَسَ مِنْهُمْ خِيفَةً ۖ قَالُوا۟ لَا تَخَفْ ۖ وَبَشَّرُوهُ بِغُلَـٰمٍ عَلِيمٍ (٢٨)
Then he felt a fear of them. They said, ‘Do not be afraid!’ and they gave him the good news of a wise son. (28)
فَأَقْبَلَتِ ٱمْرَأَتُهُۥ فِى صَرَّةٍ فَصَكَّتْ وَجْهَهَا وَقَالَتْ عَجُوزٌ عَقِيمٌ (٢٩)
Then his wife came forward crying [with joy]. She beat her face, and said, ‘A barren old woman!’ (29)
قَالُوا۟ كَذَٰلِكِ قَالَ رَبُّكِ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلْحَكِيمُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (٣٠)
They said, ‘So has your Lord said. Indeed He is the All-wise, the All-knowing.’ (30)

Commentary

The Guests of Abraham (عليه السلام)

From these verses onward, glimpses of the life stories of the Prophets and past nations are presented to emphasize and confirm the preceding points. The first segment concerns the story of the angels who appeared to Abraham (عليه السلام) in human form to punish the people of Lot, and who gave him the glad tidings of the birth of a son, even though Abraham had reached old age and his wife was also elderly and barren.

On one hand, granting this noble son at such an age to this elderly and frail father and mother is an affirmation of what was mentioned in the previous verses regarding the predestination of all provisions.

On the other hand, it is a proof of the power and capability of the Truth (Allah), and a sign among the signs of knowing God that were discussed in the preceding verses.

Thirdly, it is a glad tiding for the believing nations who are under the protection of the Truth, just as the subsequent verses—which speak of the terrifying punishment of the people of Lot—serve as a warning and threat to the faithless criminals.

First, addressing the Prophet, He says: "Has the story of Abraham’s honored guests reached you?" (هَلْ أَتَاكَ حَدِيثُ ضَيْفِ إِبْرَاهِيمَ الْمُكْرَمِينَ) [^1]. The term "honored" (mukramīn) is used either because these angels were officials of the Truth—and in verse 26 of Surah Al-Anbiya we also read regarding the angels: "Nay, they are honored servants" (بَلْ عِبَادٌ مُّكْرَمُونَ) — or because of the honors Abraham bestowed upon them, or for both reasons.

Then, describing their state, He says: "When they entered upon him and said, 'Peace!' He said, 'Peace! [You are] a people unknown!'" (إِذْ دَخَلُوا عَلَيْهِ فَقَالُوا سَلَامًا قَالَ سَلَامٌ قَوْمٌ مُّنكَرُونَ) [^2]. Some have said that Abraham (عليه السلام) spoke the matter of them being "unknown" within his heart, not openly (as such a statement might seem inconsistent with the etiquette of honoring a guest).

However, it is common for a host, while still respecting the guest, to say:

[^1]: "Guest" (ḍayf) has a descriptive meaning and applies to both singular and plural; hence it is described by mukramīn, which is plural. The claim by some that it is a verbal noun (maṣdar) and lacks dual or plural forms does not seem correct. However, as Al-Zamakhshari states in Al-Kashshaf, since it was originally a verbal noun, even after taking on a descriptive meaning, it continues to be used for both singular and plural. (Note carefully).

[^2]: Salāman is in the accusative case due to an omitted verb, estimated as "We greet you with peace" (nusallimu ‘alaykum salāman). The second Salāmun is a subject (mubtada’) whose predicate (khabar) is omitted, originally being "Peace be upon you" (‘alaykum salām). It is as if Abraham (عليه السلام) wished to give a greeting superior to theirs, as the nominal sentence (jumla ismiyya) denotes stability and permanence (Tafsir Al-Kashshaf, Vol. 4, p. 401).

"But I do not know where I have seen you? You do not seem very familiar to me." Therefore, the literal meaning of the verse can be maintained—that Abraham said this openly—though the first possibility is not far-fetched either, especially since no response from the guests identifying themselves is seen here; had Abraham spoken this openly, they likely would have responded to it.

In any case, the hospitable and generous Abraham immediately set to work to entertain his guests: "He turned to his family privately and brought a fat [roasted] calf" (فَرَاغَ إِلَىٰ أَهْلِهِ فَجَاءَ بِعِجْلٍ سَمِينٍ). The word rāgha, as Al-Raghib states in Al-Mufradat, comes from rawgh, meaning to move with a subtle or private plan.

Why did Abraham do this? Because it was possible that if the guests noticed, they might prevent such an expensive reception.

But why did Abraham prepare such an abundant and elaborate meal for a limited number of guests—who some say were three and at most twelve [^1]? This is because generous people, whenever a guest arrives, do not prepare food only for the guests; rather, they provide a meal in which all those who work for them can share, and they even consider neighbors, relatives, and others nearby. Therefore, such extra food is never considered waste (isrāf), and this practice is still seen today among many tribes and those living in traditional ways.

[^1]: Adapted from Ruh al-Bayan and the marginalia of Tafsir al-Safi under the verses in question.

‘Ijl means a calf (the claim by some that it means a sheep is inconsistent with linguistic texts). This word is originally derived from ‘ajalah (haste), because this animal at this age has hasty movements which it completely abandons when it grows up.

Samīn means fat, and the choice of such a calf was to honor the guests and to allow more people around him to benefit.

In verse 69 of Surah Hud, it is mentioned that this calf was roasted (بِعِجْلٍ حَنِيذٍ); although the verse under discussion does not mention this, it is not inconsistent with it.

"Abraham personally brought this food to the guests and placed it near them" (فَقَرَّبَهُ إِلَيْهِمْ). But to his great surprise, he observed that they did not reach for the food. "He said, 'Will you not eat?'" (قَالَ أَلَا تَأْكُلُونَ).

Abraham assumed they were human beings, "and when he saw they did not reach for the food, he felt a sense of fear within himself" (فَأَوْجَسَ مِنْهُمْ خِيفَةً). This is because at that time—and even today among many nations committed to traditional ethics—whenever someone eats another's food, they will not harm or betray them; they do not "break the salt shaker" after eating the salt. Thus, if a guest did not touch the food, the suspicion arose that they had come for a dangerous purpose. The famous Arabic proverb says: "He who does not eat your food does not keep your covenant."

Ījās comes from wajs, which originally means a hidden sound. Accordingly, ījās refers to a hidden, internal feeling, as if a person hears a voice from within themselves. When accompanied by khīfah (fear), it means a feeling of dread.

At this point, the guests—as stated in Surah Hud, verse 70—"said to him, 'Do not fear,'" and they reassured him (قَالُوا لَا تَخَفْ).

Then it adds: "And they gave him glad tidings of a knowledgeable son" (وَبَشَّرُوهُ بِغُلَامٍ عَلِيمٍ).

It is obvious that a child is not "knowledgeable" at birth, but there may be a potential within him to become a great scholar and sage in the future, and that is what is meant here.

There is debate among commentators as to whether this son was Ishmael or Isaac, though the famous view is that it was Isaac.

However, the possibility of it being Ishmael does not seem correct in light of verse 71 of Surah Hud, which says: "So We gave her glad tidings of Isaac" (فَبَشَّرْنَاهَا بِإِسْحَاقَ). Therefore, there is no doubt that the woman mentioned in the following verses is Abraham’s wife, Sarah, and this child is Isaac.

"At this moment, Abraham’s wife came forward, crying out in joy and amazement; she struck her face and said, 'Shall I have a child while I am a barren old woman?'" (فَأَقْبَلَتِ امْرَأَتُهُ فِي صَرَّةٍ فَصَكَّتْ وَجْهَهَا وَقَالَتْ عَجُوزٌ عَقِيمٌ). In verse 72 of Surah Hud, we also read: "She said, 'Woe to me! Shall I bear a child while I am an old woman, and this my husband is an old man? This is indeed a strange thing!'" Therefore, her cry was one of amazement coupled with joy.

The word ṣarrah comes from ṣarr, which originally means tying or binding together. It is also used for a loud cry and a dense crowd because of their intensity and togetherness. Intense cold winds are called ṣarṣar because they wrap people up. Ṣarūrah refers to a man or woman who has not yet performed Hajj or has no intention to marry, as there is a kind of binding or restraint in them. In the verse under discussion, it means a loud cry.

Ṣakkat comes from ṣakk, meaning to strike hard, or to strike the face. What is meant here is that when Abraham’s wife heard the news of the child's birth, she struck her face with her hands out of intense amazement and modesty, as is the habit of women.

According to some commentators, as well as the Book of Genesis in the Torah, Abraham’s wife was ninety years old or more at that time, and Abraham himself was about one hundred years old or more!

But the Quran, in the next verse, relates the angels' response to her: "They said: 'Thus has your Lord said; and He is the All-Wise, the All-Knowing'" (قَالُوا كَذَٰلِكِ قَالَ رَبُّكِ ۖ إِنَّهُ هُوَ الْحَكِيمُ الْعَلِيمُ).

Though you are a frail old woman and your husband is likewise, when the command of your Lord is issued and His will pertains to something, it will undoubtedly come to pass.

Even the creation of a vast universe like this one is easy and simple for Him with the command "Be" (kun).

The terms "All-Wise" (Al-Ḥakīm) and "All-Knowing" (Al-‘Alīm) indicate that He does not need you to inform Him of your old age and barrenness or your husband’s seniority; Allah knows all of this, and if He has not given you a child until now and grants one at the end of your lives, that too has a wisdom.

Interestingly, in verse 73 of Surah Hud, we read that the angels said to her: "Do you wonder at the decree of Allah? The mercy of Allah and His blessings be upon you, O people of the house! Indeed, He is Praiseworthy, All-Glorious."

The difference between these two expressions is because the angels said all these things to Sarah; however, in Surah Hud, one part is mentioned, and here another part. There, the talk is of Allah’s mercy and blessings, which is appropriate for "Praiseworthy and All-Glorious" (One who is praised and glorified for His favors).

But here, the talk is of Allah’s knowledge regarding the lack of physical readiness of these two spouses to have a child and the woman's barrenness in terms of outward causes. It is appropriate here to say that Allah is aware of all this, and if it is asked why He did not grant this gift in their youth, it is said: there is a wisdom in this matter, for He is All-Wise.

Key Point: The Generosity of the Prophets

It often happens that some rigid individuals mistake generosity and high-mindedness for extravagance and waste, and stinginess and narrow-mindedness for asceticism and piety.

The Quran, in the above verses and the verses of Surah Hud, clearly reveals this truth: that entertaining a guest extensively and reasonably is never contrary to the Sharia. Rather, because a Prophet performed such an act, it is proof of its merit—provided, of course, that such hospitality extends to others as well, as is the custom of noble and generous people.

Allah has never forbidden benefiting from the blessings of life, nor has He considered it a flaw to possess lawful wealth like Abraham, from which others also benefit.

With all that abundant wealth, Abraham (عليه السلام) never forgot the remembrance of Allah, never developed an enslaving attachment to it, and never restricted its benefits to himself.

The Quran says in verse 32 of Surah Al-A'raf: "Say, 'Who has forbidden the adornment of Allah which He has produced for His servants and the good [things] of provision?' Say, 'They are for those who believe during the worldly life [though others share in them] and exclusively for them on the Day of Resurrection.' Thus do We detail the verses for a people who know."

In this regard, we have provided a detailed discussion in Volume 6, page 150 onwards, under verse 32 of Al-A'raf.

Beginning of the 27th Juz' of the Holy Quran

Ayahs

۞ قَالَ فَمَا خَطْبُكُمْ أَيُّهَا ٱلْمُرْسَلُونَ (٣١)
He said, ‘O messengers, what is now your errand?’ (31)
قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّآ أُرْسِلْنَآ إِلَىٰ قَوْمٍ مُّجْرِمِينَ (٣٢)
They said, ‘We have been sent toward a guilty people, (32)
لِنُرْسِلَ عَلَيْهِمْ حِجَارَةً مِّن طِينٍ (٣٣)
that We may rain upon them stones of clay, (33)
مُّسَوَّمَةً عِندَ رَبِّكَ لِلْمُسْرِفِينَ (٣٤)
sent for the transgressors from your Lord. (34)
فَأَخْرَجْنَا مَن كَانَ فِيهَا مِنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٣٥)
So We picked out those who were in it of the faithful, (35)
فَمَا وَجَدْنَا فِيهَا غَيْرَ بَيْتٍ مِّنَ ٱلْمُسْلِمِينَ (٣٦)
but We did not find there other than one house of Muslims, (36)
وَتَرَكْنَا فِيهَآ ءَايَةً لِّلَّذِينَ يَخَافُونَ ٱلْعَذَابَ ٱلْأَلِيمَ (٣٧)
and We have left therein a sign for those who fear a painful punishment.’ (37)

Commentary

The Afflicted Cities of the People of Lut are a Sign and a Lesson

Following the account of the angels' arrival to Ibrahim and their glad tidings to him regarding the birth of Ishaq, the discussion turns to the conversation that took place between Ibrahim and the angels concerning the people of Lut.

To clarify: After Ibrahim was exiled to Sham (the Levant), he continued inviting people toward Allah and struggling against every form of polytheism and idol-worship. Prophet Lut (peace be upon him), who was among the great prophets, lived during his era and was likely commissioned by him to travel to one of the regions of Sham (namely the cities of Sodom) to preach and guide the wayward. He came among a sinful people who were mired in polytheism and many transgressions, the most heinous of which was the sin of homosexuality and sodomy. Eventually, a group of angels was commissioned to destroy this people, but they first visited Ibrahim.

From the state of his guests, Ibrahim realized they were pursuing an important matter and had not come solely to bring glad tidings of a child's birth, as one person would have sufficed for such news; or perhaps due to their haste in movement, he sensed they had a significant mission.

Therefore, in the first verse under discussion, it says: "Ibrahim said: 'What then is your business and important mission, O messengers of Allah?'" (قَالَ فَمَا خَطْبُكُمْ أَيُّهَا الْمُرْسَلُونَ) [^1]

The angels unveiled their mission and "said to Ibrahim: 'We have been sent to a criminal and wicked people'" (قَالُوا إِنَّا أُرْسِلْنَا إِلَىٰ قَوْمٍ مُجْرِمِينَ).

A people who, in addition to corruption in belief, were afflicted with various types of pollution and different foul and shameful sins. [^1]

Then they added: "We are commissioned to send down upon them a rain of 'stones of clay'" to crush and destroy them thereby (لِنُرْسِلَ عَلَيْهِمْ حِجَارَةً مِنْ طِينٍ). The expression "حِجَارَةً مِنْ طِينٍ" (stones of clay) is the same thing referred to as "Sijjil" in verse 82 of Surah Hud. "Sijjil" is originally a Persian word derived from "Sang" (stone) and "Gil" (clay), which entered the Arabic language as "Sijjil." Thus, it is something that is neither as hard as stone nor as soft as clay. Collectively, it perhaps points to the fact that to destroy this criminal people, there was not even a need to send down massive boulders from the sky; rather, a rain of small pebbles, not particularly firm, fell upon them like raindrops.

Then they added: "These stones are marked from your Lord for the transgressors" (مُسَوَّمَةً عِنْدَ رَبِّكَ لِلْمُسْرِفِينَ).

[^1]: It should be noted that "Khatb" is not used for just any task, but rather means important matters, whereas "shughl" (job), "amr" (affair), "fi'l" (action), and the like have a general meaning.

[^1]: It is noteworthy that in Surah Hud, when mentioning this event, it says: "We have been sent to the people of Lut." This difference in expressions between the verses under discussion and the verses of Surah Hud is because each points to a part of the event; in other words, all these matters occurred, but some are mentioned within the verses under discussion and others in other Surahs.

"Musawwamah" refers to something that possesses a mark or sign. There is discussion among commentators as to how they were marked. Some have said: They had a specific shape indicating they were not ordinary stones but instruments of punishment. A group has said: Each one had a mark and was aimed at a specific individual and a particular spot, so that people would know Allah's punishments are so calculated that it is even known which criminal individual must be destroyed by which stone!

The term "Musrifin" (transgressors/extravagant) refers to the abundance of their sins, such that they had exceeded all bounds and torn the veils of modesty and shame. If one reflects on the conditions of the people of Lut and their various sins, they will see that this expression is very meaningful regarding them. [^1] Any human might occasionally become tainted by a sin, but if they soon awaken and compensate and reform, it is not so difficult; the problem arises when one turns to "Israf" (excess/transgression).

At the same time, this expression conveys another point: that not only were these heavenly stones aimed at the people of Lut, but they lie in wait for all transgressing sinners.

Here, the Quran leaves the continuation of the story of these messengers of the Lord—who came to Prophet Lut (peace be upon him) and entered upon him as guests, and the shameless people came after them thinking they were handsome young men of human kind, but soon realized their mistake and all their eyes were blinded—and recounts the rest of the matter from Allah's perspective. [^1] It says: "We brought out all the believers who were living in the cities of the people of Lut before the descent of the calamity" (فَأَخْرَجْنَا مَنْ كَانَ فِيهَا مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ).

"But in all these regions, We found none but one Muslim household!" (فَمَا وَجَدْنَا فِيهَا غَيْرَ بَيْتٍ مِنَ الْمُسْلِمِينَ). Yes, We never burn the dry and the wet together, and Our justice does not permit making the believer suffer the fate of the disbeliever; even if among millions of faithless and criminal people there is one faithful and pure individual, We save him.

This is the same point mentioned in Surah Al-Hijr, verses 59 and 60, in this form: "Except the family of Lut; indeed, we will save them all, except his wife; we decreed that she is of those who remain behind (to be destroyed)!" And in Surah Hud, verse 81, we read: "Travel with your family in a part of the night, and let none of you look back, except your wife; indeed, that which strikes them will strike her!".

[^1]: Refer to Volume 9, page 197 (under verse 81 of Surah Hud).

[^1]: This point is noteworthy: in the verses of Surah Hud, this event is narrated, but the expressions clearly show that the meeting of these messengers with Ibrahim was before the punishment of the people of Lut, whereas in the verses under discussion, there are expressions showing this meeting took place afterward. The solution to this issue is as follows: up to the verse "marked from your Lord for the transgressors," it is the speech of the angels, but the three verses after it mentioned above are the words of Allah, addressed to the Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family) and the Muslims, speaking of it as an event that took place in the past (observe closely).

And in Surah Al-Ankabut, verse 32, this same occurrence is recounted as follows: "Ibrahim said: 'Indeed, Lut is in it.' They said: 'We know better who is in it; we will surely save him and his family, except his wife; she was of those who remain behind.'"

And again, this subject is reflected in Surah Al-A'raf, verse 83, as such: "So We saved him and his family, except his wife; she was of those who remained behind (and met their fate)." As you observe, this part of the story of the people of Lut is expressed in these five Surahs of the Quran with different phrasings, all of which recount one reality. However, since an event can be viewed from different angles and each view reveals a dimension of it, historical events in the Holy Quran are often presented and repeated in this manner, to which the different expressions of the above verses testify. Furthermore, because the Quran is a book of education and human-building, in the position of education, it sometimes becomes necessary to emphasize an important issue many times to leave a deep impact on the reader's mind; however, this repetition must be done with interesting, pleasant, and varied expressions so that no boredom occurs and it remains eloquent and articulate.

(For more explanation on the story of Ibrahim's guests (peace be upon him) and his conversations with these guests, and then the painful and exemplary fate of the people of Lut, please refer to Tafsir Nemouneh, Volume 6, page 243 onwards; Volume 9, page 167; Volume 11, page 98; and Volume 16, page 260, under the verses of Surah Al-A'raf, Hud, Al-Hijr, and Al-Ankabut).

In any case, Allah overturned the cities of these polluted people with a severe and devastating earthquake, then showered them with a rain of heavenly stones, and no trace of them remained; even their foul corpses were buried under the rubble and heavenly stones, to be a lesson for future generations and for all faithless individuals and polluted criminals.

Therefore, in the final verse under discussion, it adds: "And We left therein a clear sign for those who fear the painful punishment" (وَتَرَكْنَا فِيهَا آيَةً لِلَّذِينَ يَخَافُونَ الْعَذَابَ الْأَلِيمَ). This expression clearly shows that only those who have the readiness to accept within themselves and feel a sense of responsibility take heed from these signs and tokens of Allah.

Key Point: Where is the location of the cities of Lut?

It is certain that Ibrahim, after migrating from Iraq and the land of Babylon, came toward the Levant (Shamat). It is said that Lut also lived with him, but after a while (to invite to monotheism and struggle against corruption), he went to the city of "Sodom."

"Sodom" is the name of one of the cities and settlements of the people of Lut, which was located in the Levant (in the country of Jordan) near the Dead Sea. It was a prosperous land full of trees and vegetation, but after the descent of divine punishment upon this wicked and shameful people, their cities were crushed and overturned, such that they are called "Mada'in Mu'tafikat" (the overturned cities).

Some believe that the ruins of these cities have sunk under the water and claim that in a corner of the Dead Sea lake, they have seen pillars and other remains indicating the ruins of this city.

And the fact that we see in some Islamic commentaries that the meaning of the phrase "And We left therein a sign" is those foul waters that have covered the site of these cities might point to this same meaning: that after the severe earthquakes and the splitting of the earth, a path was opened from the Dead Sea to this afflicted land, and all these cities sank under the water! While others believe the cities of Lut did not sink under water, and currently near the Dead Sea, there is a region covered under black stones which is likely the site of the cities of Lut.

It is also said that Ibrahim's center was in the city of "Hebron" (Al-Khalil) at a distance not far from "Sodom," and when their cities caught fire due to the earthquake or lightning, Ibrahim was standing near Hebron and saw the smoke of the city rising with his own eyes. [^1] From the sum of these accounts, the approximate boundaries of these cities become clear, although the details still remain shrouded in a veil of ambiguity.

[^1]: Adapted from the book "Qamus-e Moqaddas," "Dehkhoda Encyclopedia," and "Al-Munjid" (Biographies section).

Ayahs

وَفِى مُوسَىٰٓ إِذْ أَرْسَلْنَـٰهُ إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ بِسُلْطَـٰنٍ مُّبِينٍ (٣٨)

مَا تَذَرُ مِن شَىْءٍ أَتَتْ عَلَيْهِ إِلَّا جَعَلَتْهُ كَٱلرَّمِيمِ (٤٢)
