وَفِى ثَمُودَ إِذْ قِيلَ لَهُمْ تَمَتَّعُوا۟ حَتَّىٰ حِينٍ (٤٣)
And in Thamud, when they were told, ‘Enjoy for a while.’ (43)
فَعَتَوْا۟ عَنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّهِمْ فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ ٱلصَّـٰعِقَةُ وَهُمْ يَنظُرُونَ (٤٤)
Then they defied the command of their Lord; so the thunderbolt seized them as they looked on. (44)
فَمَا ٱسْتَطَـٰعُوا۟ مِن قِيَامٍ وَمَا كَانُوا۟ مُنتَصِرِينَ (٤٥)
So they were neither able to rise up, nor to come to one another’s aid. (45)
وَقَوْمَ نُوحٍ مِّن قَبْلُ ۖ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ قَوْمًا فَـٰسِقِينَ (٤٦)
And the people of Noah aforetime. Indeed, they were a transgressing lot. (46)

Commentary

So Many Lessons in the History of the Predecessors

In these verses, following the story of the people of Lot and the painful end they met due to their extremely foul and shameful sins, the Quran refers to the history of several other ancient nations.

First, It says: "And in Moses [there was a sign], when We sent him to Pharaoh with clear authority" (وَ فِي مُوسى‌ إِذْ أَرْسَلْناهُ إِلى‌ فِرْعَوْنَ بِسُلْطانٍ مُبِينٍ‌).

"Sultan" (authority) refers to something that grants dominance, and here it means a miracle, or powerful intellectual logic and reasoning, or both, which Moses (عليه السلام) utilized against Pharaoh.

The expression "Sultan Mubin" (clear authority) is used frequently in various verses of the Quran, and it generally signifies a clear and manifest logical proof.

However, Pharaoh neither submitted to the great miracles of Moses (عليه السلام), which testified to his connection with God, nor did he bow before his logical arguments. Instead, due to his pride and arrogance, "he turned away with his supporters and said, 'A magician or a madman!'" (فَتَوَلَّى بِرُكْنِهِ وَ قالَ ساحِرٌ أَوْ مَجْنُونٌ‌).

"Rukn" (pillar/cornerstone) originally means a pillar, a main support, or an essential part of anything. Here, it might refer to the limbs of the body, meaning Pharaoh turned his back on Moses completely with his entire being.

Some have also said it refers to his army, meaning he relied on the pillars of his military and turned away from the message of truth.

Alternatively, it could mean that he himself turned away from Allah's command and also led the pillars of his government and army astray. [^1] Interestingly, the oppressive tyrants were caught in a strange state of confusion, contradiction, and rambling in the false accusations they leveled against the great prophets. Sometimes they called them magicians, and sometimes madmen—even though a "magician" must be an alert and clever person who uses subtleties, psychological issues, and the various properties of objects to perform amazing feats and deceive people, whereas a madman is the exact opposite.

But the Quran reports the outcome of the tyrant Pharaoh and his helpers as follows: "So We

[^1]: It should be noted that the "Ba" in "bi-ruknihi," according to the first interpretation, is the "Ba of accompaniment" (musahabah); according to the second, it is the "Ba of causality" (sababiyyah); and according to the third, it is the "Ba of transition" (ta'diyah).

took him and his hosts and flung them into the sea, while he was blameworthy" (فأَخَذْناهُ وَ جُنُودَهُ فَنَبَذْناهُمْ فِي الْيَمِّ وَ هُوَ مُلِيمٌ‌). [^1] "Yamm," as understood from linguistic texts and books of Hadith, means "sea," and it is also applied to massive rivers like the Nile. [^2] The expression "nabadhnahum" (We flung them) indicates that Pharaoh, with all his power and armies, was so weak before the will of Allah that He cast them into the middle of the Nile like an insignificant object.

The expression "wa huwa mulim" (and he was blameworthy) indicates that not only did divine punishment wipe out this people, but the history that remains of them has preserved their shameful name and disgraceful deeds, exposing their oppression, crime, pride, and arrogance such that they are forever deserving of blame.

Then, it addresses the brief fate of another people, namely the people of "Ad," saying: "And in Ad [there was a sign], when We sent against them the barren wind" (وَ فِي عادٍ إِذْ أَرْسَلْنا عَلَيْهِمُ الرِّيحَ الْعَقِيمَ‌).

[^1]: "Mulim" is the active participle from the form "If'al" from the root "lawm," meaning blame. In such contexts, it refers to one who commits an act deserving of blame, similar to "mughrib," meaning one who performs strange and wondrous acts.

[^2]: Further explanation on this matter is provided in Volume 6, page 327, under verse 136 of Surah Al-A'raf.

A wind is "barren" (aqim) when it does not carry rain-bearing clouds, does not pollinate plants, possesses no benefit or blessing, and brings nothing but destruction and annihilation.

Then, describing the gale that overcame the people of Ad, it adds: "It spared nothing that it came upon, but turned it into crumbled dust" (ما تَذَرُ مِنْ شَيْ‌ءٍ أَتَتْ عَلَيْهِ إِلَّا جَعَلَتْهُ كَالرَّمِيمِ‌).

"Ramim" comes from the root "rammah" (rhyming with 'minnah'), which refers to decayed bones. "Rimah" (rhyming with 'qubbah') refers to a decayed rope, and "ramm" (rhyming with 'jinn') refers to the tiny fragments of wood or straw that fall to the ground. [^1] "Ramm" and "tarmim" also mean to repair decayed things. [^2] This expression shows that the gale of the people of Ad was no ordinary wind; rather, in addition to destruction, crushing, and physical pressure, it possessed a burning and toxic quality that caused various objects to decay.

Indeed, such is the power of Allah that with a swift movement of a "breeze," He crushes powerful and boisterous nations such that only decayed corpses remain of them. This was a brief reference to the fate of the powerful and wealthy people of "Ad" who lived in the land of "Ahqaf" (a region between Oman and Hadramaut).

[^1]: Raghib's Mufradat (root: r-m).

[^2]: Lisan al-Arab and Mufradat (root: r-m).

After them, it is the turn of the people of "Thamud," and regarding them, He says: "And in Thamud [there was a sign], when it was said to them, 'Enjoy yourselves for a while'" (وَ فِي ثَمُودَ إِذْ قِيلَ لَهُمْ تَمَتَّعُوا حَتَّى حِينٍ‌). The meaning of "for a while" (hatta hin) is the three-day respite mentioned in verse 65 of Surah Hud: "But they hamstrung her. So he said, 'Enjoy yourselves in your homes for three days. That is a promise not to be belied.'"

Even though Allah had warned them many times through His prophet Salih (عليه السلام), they were given a three-day respite for further completion of the proof (itmam al-hujjah), so they might compensate for their dark past and wash the rust of sin from their hearts and souls with the water of repentance. According to some commentators, during these three days, changes appeared in their skin: first it turned yellow, then red, and finally black, as warnings for this rebellious polytheistic people. But unfortunately, none of these matters availed them, and they did not descend from the steed of pride.

Indeed, "they were insolent toward the command of their Lord, so the thunderbolt took them while they were looking on, and they were unable to rise, nor could they help themselves" (فَعَتَوْا عَنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّهِمْ فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ الصَّاعِقَةُ وَ هُمْ يَنْظُرُونَ‌). "Ataw" comes from the root "utuww" (rhyming with 'ghuluww'), meaning to refuse to obey.

It appears that this sentence refers to all the acts of defiance they committed throughout Salih's mission, such as idol-worship, oppression, and killing the she-camel that was the miracle of Salih (عليه السلام), not just the defiance during those three days when they remained immersed in heedlessness and pride instead of repenting and turning to Allah.

Evidence for this is verse 77 of Surah Al-A'raf, which says: "So they hamstrung the she-camel and were insolent toward the command of their Lord and said, 'O Salih, bring us what you threaten us with, if you are of the messengers!'" "Sa'iqah" and "saq'ah" are close in meaning, originally signifying a collapse accompanied by a loud noise, with the difference that "sa'iqah" is used for celestial bodies and "saq'ah" for terrestrial ones. According to some linguists, "sa'iqah" sometimes means "death," sometimes "punishment," and sometimes "fire." This word is mostly applied to the intense sound that rises from the sky accompanied by deadly fire, encompassing all three meanings (death, punishment, and fire).

We have previously mentioned that when clouds carrying positive electricity approach ground that has negative electricity, a massive electrical spark arises between the two, accompanied by a terrifying sound and scorching fire, causing the location of its occurrence to tremble.

In the Holy Quran, in verse 19 of Surah Al-Baqarah, it is clearly used in this sense, for after speaking of clouds, rain, thunder, and lightning, it adds: "They put their fingers in their ears against the thunderclaps in dread of death" (يَجْعَلُونَ أَصابِعَهُمْ فِي آذانِهِمْ مِنَ الصَّواعِقِ حَذَرَ الْمَوْتِ‌).

Finally, the last sentence He says regarding this rebellious people is: "So they were unable to rise, nor could they help themselves" (فَمَا اسْتَطاعُوا مِنْ قِيامٍ وَ ما كانُوا مُنْتَصِرِينَ‌). Indeed, the thunderbolt caught them so off-guard and crushed them to the ground that they had neither the strength to rise and defend themselves, nor the power to wail, cry out, or seek help; they died in that very state, and their story became a lesson for others.

Yes, the people of Thamud, who were among the famous Arab tribes and lived in the land of "Hijr" (a region in northern Hejaz) with abundant resources, wealth, long lives, and sturdy buildings, were destroyed due to their defiance of Allah's command, rebellion, transgression, polytheism, and oppression, and their ruins became an eloquent lesson for others.

In the final verse under discussion, He makes a brief reference to the fate of the fifth people, the people of Noah, saying: "And the people of Noah before [them]; indeed, they were a defiantly disobedient people" (وَ قَوْمَ نُوحٍ مِنْ قَبْلُ إِنَّهُمْ كانُوا قَوْماً فاسِقِينَ‌). [^1] A "fasiq" (defiantly disobedient) is one who steps outside the bounds of Allah's command and becomes tainted with disbelief, oppression, or other sins. The expression "min qabl" (before them) perhaps indicates that the people of Pharaoh,

[^1]: This sentence has an omitted verb, and according to Zamakhshari in "Al-Kashshaf," it is estimated as: "And We destroyed the people of Noah," although "We destroyed" was not in the preceding verses, it is clearly understood from their context.

Lot, Ad, and Thamud had heard the tragic story of the people of Noah who preceded them, but unfortunately, it did not awaken them, and they met a fate similar to theirs.

Key Points

1- The Various Faces of Divine Punishment

It is noteworthy that in the above verses and the preceding ones, reference is made to the history of five nations of the past (the people of Lot, Pharaoh, Ad, Thamud, and the people of Noah). Among them, the punishment of the first four is mentioned, but the punishment of the people of Noah is not explicitly detailed here. When we look closely, we see that each of the first four nations was punished by one of the "four elements": the people of "Lot" were destroyed by earthquake, debris, and heavenly stones (i.e., earth); the people of Pharaoh by "water"; the people of "Ad" by "gale" (wind); and the people of "Thamud" by "thunderbolt and fire."

It is true that these four things are not recognized today as "elements" (i.e., simple substances), as each is a combination of other substances, but it cannot be denied that they constitute four important pillars of human life. If any one of them were completely removed from human life, the continuation of life would be impossible, let alone all of them.

Indeed, Allah placed the death and destruction of these nations in the very thing that was the primary factor of their life, without which they could not continue to exist; and this is a strange ultimate power.

And if no mention was made of the factor of punishment for the people of Noah (عليه السلام), it is perhaps because their punishment, like that of the people of Pharaoh, was by "water" and did not require repetition here.

2- "Fertilizing" and "Barren" Winds

In the above verses, we read that Allah punished the people of Ad with a barren and sterile gale, while in verse 22 of Surah "Al-Hijr" we read: "And We have sent the fertilizing winds and sent down water from the sky" (وَ أَرْسَلْنَا الرِّياحَ لَواقِحَ فَأَنْزَلْنا مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً).

Although this verse primarily refers to the pollination of clouds and their joining together for the descent of rain, it clarifies the role of winds in human life in general. Yes, their job is to fertilize—fertilizing clouds, plants, and even occasionally being effective in preparing various types of animals for "fertility."

But this same wind, when it carries the command of punishment, instead of creating life, becomes a factor of death and destruction. As the Quran says in verse 20 of Surah "Al-Qamar," which also speaks of the people of Ad: "Plucking away the people as if they were trunks of uprooted palm trees" (تَنْزِعُ النَّاسَ كَأَنَّهُمْ أَعْجازُ نَخْلٍ مُنْقَعِرٍ). It uprooted them (who had tall statures and large frames) from the ground and smashed them headfirst so that their heads were severed from their bodies, like a palm tree that has been uprooted.

Ayahs

وَٱلسَّمَآءَ بَنَيْنَـٰهَا بِأَيْي۟دٍ وَإِنَّا لَمُوسِعُونَ (٤٧)
We have built the heaven with might, and indeed it is We who are its expanders. (47)
وَٱلْأَرْضَ فَرَشْنَـٰهَا فَنِعْمَ ٱلْمَـٰهِدُونَ (٤٨)
And We have spread out the earth, so how excellent spreaders We have been! (48)
وَمِن كُلِّ شَىْءٍ خَلَقْنَا زَوْجَيْنِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ (٤٩)
In all things We have created pairs so that you may take admonition. (49)
فَفِرُّوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ ۖ إِنِّى لَكُم مِّنْهُ نَذِيرٌ مُّبِينٌ (٥٠)
‘So flee toward Allah. Indeed, I am from Him a manifest warner to you. (50)
وَلَا تَجْعَلُوا۟ مَعَ ٱللَّهِ إِلَـٰهًا ءَاخَرَ ۖ إِنِّى لَكُم مِّنْهُ نَذِيرٌ مُّبِينٌ (٥١)
Do not set up another god besides Allah. Indeed I am from Him a manifest warner to you.’ (51)

Commentary

We Continually Expand the Heavens!

Once again, these verses address the signs of Allah’s greatness in the world of creation. In reality, they complete the discussions found in verses 20 and 21 of this same Surah regarding His signs on the earth and within the human existence. Simultaneously, they serve as proof of Allah’s power over the matter of the Resurrection and life after death. First, He says: "We built the heaven with power, and We are steadily expanding it" (وَ السَّماءَ بَنَيْناها بِأَيْدٍ وَ إِنَّا لَمُوسِعُونَ).

"And the earth We have spread out, and how excellent a spreader are We!" (وَ الْأَرْضَ فَرَشْناها فَنِعْمَ الْماهِدُونَ). "Ayd" (rhyming with sayd) means power and strength, and it appears frequently with this meaning in the verses of the Holy Quran. Here, it refers to the perfect power of the Great Allah in the creation of the heavens.

The signs of this immense power are clearly manifest both in the vastness of the heavens and in the specific system that governs them [1].

[1]: Here, some errors have occurred for certain commentators and others, which seem necessary to mention:

1- Some commentators have interpreted "ayd" with two meanings: "power" and "blessing," whereas linguistically "ayd" means power. However, "yad" (hand), whose plural is "aydi" and its plural of plural is "ayadi," has been used to mean both power and blessing. (In the commentary of verse 17 of Surah Sad, following the late Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan, we also mentioned two meanings for "ayd," which we hereby correct).

2- In Al-Mu'jam al-Mufahras (by Muhammad Fuad Abdul Baqi), the verse under discussion is listed under the root "ayid" (with an extra 'ya') and separated from the root "ayd." This mistake apparently stems from the calligraphy of some copies of the Quran; otherwise, all commentators, as far as we know, are in consensus that the verse refers to "ayd" meaning power.

Regarding what is meant by "إِنَّا لَمُوسِعُونَ" (We are steadily expanding) here, there is discussion among the commentators: some have considered it to mean the expansion of sustenance from Allah to His servants through the falling of rain; some consider it to mean the expansion of sustenance in every aspect; and some have interpreted it as the self-sufficiency and richness of Allah, for His treasures are so vast that they never end or diminish by granting sustenance to the creation.

However, considering the mention of the creation of the heavens in the preceding phrase, and in light of recent scientific discoveries regarding the "expansion of the universe"—which has also been confirmed through sensory observations—a more subtle meaning can be found for the verse: that Allah created the heavens and is constantly expanding them.

Modern science states: not only is the planet Earth gradually becoming "fatter" and heavier due to the absorption of celestial matter, but the heavens are also in a state of expansion. That is, the stars located within a galaxy are rapidly moving away from the center of the galaxy; the speed of this expansion has even been measured in many instances.

In the book Frontiers of Astronomy by Fred Hoyle, we read: "The fastest recession speed of celestial bodies measured so far is nearly 66,000 kilometers per second! Distant galaxies appear so dim to us that measuring their speed is difficult due to insufficient light. Images taken of the sky clearly show this important discovery: that the distance of these galaxies increases much faster than that of nearby galaxies." [1] The aforementioned author then examines this speed in the "Virgo Supercluster," "Corona Borealis,"

[1]: Frontiers of Astronomy, translated by Reza Aqsa, pp. 338-340.

and "Hydra" and others, and after calculation, presents astonishing and dizzying speeds in this regard [1]. Hear also a few sentences on this subject from Mr. John Elder; he says:

"The newest and most precise measurements of the wavelengths emitted from stars have pulled back the curtain on a strange and startling fact: they have shown that the collection of stars of which the universe is composed is constantly moving away from a center at high speed, and the further their distance from this center, the more their speed increases. It is as if at one time all the stars were gathered in this center, and after that, they broke apart, and large clusters of stars separated from them and headed rapidly in every direction! Scientists have deduced from this that the universe had a starting point [2]." George Gamow, in the book The Creation of the Universe, says in this regard:

"The space of the universe, which is composed of billions of galaxies, is in a state of rapid expansion. The truth is that our universe is not at rest, but its expansion is certain.

Realizing that our universe is expanding provides the master key to the treasury of cosmological enigmas, for if the universe is currently expanding, it follows that at one time it must have been in a state of extreme contraction!" [3] It is not only the aforementioned scientists who have confessed to this truth; others have also included this meaning in their writings, the citation of which

[1]: Frontiers of Astronomy, translated by Reza Aqsa, pp. 338-340.

[2] and [3]: The Beginning and End of the Universe, pp. 74-77 (summarized).

would be lengthy.

It is noteworthy that the expression "إِنَّا لَمُوسِعُونَ" (We are expanders), using a nominal sentence and a present participle, is evidence of the continuity of this matter and shows that this expansion has always existed and continues to do so. This is exactly what has been reached today: that all celestial bodies and galaxies were initially gathered in a single center (with an extraordinarily heavy specific weight), then a massive and infinitely terrifying explosion occurred, following which the components of the universe shattered, took the form of spheres, and are in a state of rapid recession and expansion.

As for the creation of the earth and the expression "mahidun," it is a subtle term showing that Allah has "prepared" (mammahad) and readied the earth with all the means of comfort for human life. This is because "mahid" is from the root "mahd," meaning a cradle or any place prepared for rest. Such a place must be calm, secure, protected, warm, and soft, and all these conditions are met on the planet Earth.

By Divine command, on one hand, rocks have softened and turned into soil, and on the other hand, the solidity of the mountains and the hard crust of the earth have made it resistant to the pressure of tides. Thirdly, the layer of atmosphere surrounding it both retains the sun's heat—lying like a great quilt over this spread bed—and serves as a powerful shield against the onslaught of meteors, which it ignites and turns to ash as soon as they enter the Earth's domain.

In this way, all conditions for comfort have been provided by Allah to host man, who is the guest of God on this earthly sphere.

After the creation of the heavens and the earth, it is the turn of the various celestial and terrestrial beings and the types of plants and animals. Regarding this, the next verse says: "And of everything We have created pairs, that perhaps you may take heed" (وَ مِنْ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ خَلَقْنا زَوْجَيْنِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ).

Many commentators have taken "zawj" (pair) here to mean "different types," and consider the above verse to refer to the various categories of beings in this world that come in "pairs," such as night and day, light and darkness, sea and desert, sun and moon, male and female, and others.

However, as we have previously stated under similar verses, "pairing" (zawjiyyah) in such verses can refer to a more precise meaning. The word "zawj" is usually applied to the two genders "male" and "female," whether in the animal kingdom or plants. Whenever we expand it slightly, it includes all "positive" and "negative" forces. Given that the Quran says in the above verse: "مِنْ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ" (of everything), not just living beings, it can refer to the fact that all things in the world are made of positive and negative particles. Today, it is scientifically certain that "atoms" are composed of various parts, including parts that have a negative electrical charge called "electrons," and parts that have a "positive" electrical charge called protons.

Therefore, it is not mandatory to interpret "thing" (shay') strictly as an animal or plant, or to consider "pair" (zawj) as "type" (in this regard, we have mentioned other explanations in Volume 15, page 190, under verse 7 of Ash-Shu'ara; in Volume 10, page 115; and in Volume 18, page 376). It should be noted that both interpretations can be reconciled simultaneously.

Furthermore, the phrase "لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ" (that perhaps you may take heed) indicates that pairing, multiplicity, and duality in all things of the world remind man that the Creator of the universe is One and Unique, for duality is a characteristic of the created.

In a Hadith from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام, this meaning is also pointed out, where he says:

"By His making things opposites, it is known that He has no opposite; by His pairing things together, it is known that He has no peer. He made light the opposite of darkness, dryness the opposite of wetness, roughness the opposite of softness, and cold the opposite of heat. He joined together things that are hostile to each other and separated things that are close, so that their separation points to their Separator and their joining points to their Joiner. And that is the meaning of: 'And of everything We have created pairs, that perhaps you may take heed'" [1].

In the next verse, as a conclusion to the preceding monotheistic discussions, it adds: "So flee to Allah; indeed, I am to you from Him a clear warner!" (فَفِرُّوا إِلَى اللَّهِ إِنِّي لَكُمْ مِنْهُ نَذِيرٌ مُبِينٌ).

The expression "flee" (firru) here is an interesting and subtle one. Usually, "fleeing" is said when a person is faced with a terrifying being or event on one hand, and knows of a sanctuary at a certain point on the other; thus, they move away from the site of the event with full speed

[1]: Tawhid al-Saduq, as quoted in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 130.

and turn toward the point of safety and security. You too, flee from polytheism and idol-worship—which is a terrifying belief—and turn toward pure monotheism, which is the real zone of safety and security.

Flee from the punishment of Allah and go toward His mercy. Flee from disobediences and sins and resort to repentance and turning back to Him. In short, flee from ugliness, evils, lack of faith, the darkness of ignorance, and eternal punishment, and place yourselves in the embrace of Divine mercy and eternal happiness.

Again, for further emphasis, it stresses the issue of monotheism, saying: "And do not set up with Allah another deity; indeed, I am to you from Him a clear warner" (وَ لا تَجْعَلُوا مَعَ اللَّهِ إِلهاً آخَرَ إِنِّي لَكُمْ مِنْهُ نَذِيرٌ مُبِينٌ).

The possibility exists that the previous verse invites to the principle of faith in Allah, and this verse invites to the Oneness of His Pure Essence. Therefore, the repetition of the phrase "إِنِّي لَكُمْ مِنْهُ نَذِيرٌ مُبِينٌ" in one instance is as a "warning" against abandoning faith in Allah, and in the other instance, it is a warning against polytheism and dualism; thus, each refers to a separate matter.

In some narrations from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, it is mentioned that the meaning of "fleeing to Allah" is the Hajj and visiting His House [1]. It is clear that the intention is to mention one of the obvious examples of fleeing to Allah, for Hajj acquaints man with the reality of monotheism, repentance, and turning back to Him, and places him under the protection of Divine grace.

[1]: In this regard, several Hadiths from Imam al-Baqir (ع) and Imam al-Sadiq (ع) are quoted in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, pp. 130 and 131.

Ayahs

كَذَٰلِكَ مَآ أَتَى ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِم مِّن رَّسُولٍ إِلَّا قَالُوا۟ سَاحِرٌ أَوْ مَجْنُونٌ (٥٢)
So it was that there did not come any apostle to those who were before them but they said, ‘A magician,’ or ‘A crazy man!’ (52)
أَتَوَاصَوْا۟ بِهِۦ ۚ بَلْ هُمْ قَوْمٌ طَاغُونَ (٥٣)
Did they enjoin this upon one another?! Rather, they were a rebellious lot. (53)
فَتَوَلَّ عَنْهُمْ فَمَآ أَنتَ بِمَلُومٍ (٥٤)
So turn away from them, as you will not be blameworthy. (54)
وَذَكِّرْ فَإِنَّ ٱلذِّكْرَىٰ تَنفَعُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٥٥)
And admonish, for admonition indeed benefits the faithful. (55)

Commentary

Remind, for Reminding is Beneficial

In verse 39 of this very Surah, we read that in response to the invitation of Musa عليه السلام toward the One God and the abandonment of oppression and injustice, Pharaoh accused Musa of being either a "sorcerer" or "madman." This same accusation, which was also leveled against the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله by the polytheists, was very heavy for the few early believers and grieved the pure soul of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

The verses under discussion offer consolation to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the believers, saying: You are not the only one targeted by these poisoned arrows of slander; "Similarly, no messenger came to those before them but they said: [He is] a sorcerer or a madman" (كَذلِكَ ما أَتَى الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ مِنْ رَسُولٍ إِلَّا قالُوا ساحِرٌ أَوْ مَجْنُونٌ) ^1. They called them "sorcerers" because they had no logical response to their remarkable miracles, and they addressed them as "madmen" because they did not conform to their environment and did not bow down to material privileges.

Therefore, do not worry and do not allow grief and sorrow into your heart; increase your steadfastness, persistence, and patience, for such baseless words and attributions have always stood against men of truth.

Then He adds: "Did they suggest it to them?" (أَ تَواصَوْا بِهِ‌)—meaning, did these disbelieving and antagonistic nations recommend to one another to cast this slander upon all the prophets? They act so harmoniously and uniformly that it is as if they all held a meeting beyond the reaches of history, sat in consultation, and advised one another to generally accuse the prophets of sorcery and madness, so as to diminish their influence and credibility among the masses.

And perhaps as each generation was about to depart from this world, they whispered this into the ears of their children and friends, recommending it to them.

Then He adds: "Rather, they are a transgressing people" (بَلْ هُمْ قَوْمٌ طاغُونَ) ^1. This is the effect of a rebellious spirit; to drive the men of truth from the scene, they resort to every lie and slander. Since prophets come among nations with miracles and new commands, they find the best label to be accusing them of "sorcery" and "madness." Therefore, the factor behind their "unity of action" is their single, wicked spirit of transgression.

Again, to further comfort and console the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, He says: "So turn away from them" (فَتَوَلَّ عَنْهُمْ), now that this rebellious and defiant people are unwilling to hear the truth. And be certain that you have fulfilled your duty completely, and "you are not to be blamed" (فَما أَنْتَ بِمَلُومٍ).

If they do not accept the truth, do not grieve, for worthy hearts are waiting for it.

This sentence is, in fact, a reminder of other verses showing that the Prophet was so compassionate that he was sometimes close to dying of grief over their lack of faith, as we read in Surah Al-Kahf, verse 6: "Then perhaps you would kill yourself through grief over them, if they do not believe in this message, out of sorrow" (فَلَعَلَّكَ باخِعٌ نَفْسَكَ عَلى‌ آثارِهِمْ إِنْ لَمْ يُؤْمِنُوا بِهذَا الْحَدِيثِ أَسَفاً).

Indeed, a true leader must be such.

Commentators have said that when this verse was revealed, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the believers became sorrowful, imagining that this was the final word regarding the polytheists, that heavenly revelation had ceased, and that divine punishment would soon arrive. However, it was not long before the next verse was revealed, commanding the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "And remind, for indeed, the reminder benefits the believers" (وَ ذَكِّرْ فَإِنَّ الذِّكْرى‌ تَنْفَعُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ).

It was then that everyone felt a sense of peace ^1, an indication that there are ready hearts in various corners waiting for your words. If one group has risen in opposition to the truth, another group yearns for it with all their soul, and your pleasant speech will leave its mark upon their souls.

Key Point: Ready Hearts are Necessary for Accepting the Truth

Consider a farmer busy sowing seeds. He might scatter some of these seeds upon rocks; certainly, they will never bear fruit.

Another portion falls on thin layers of soil covering hard stones; here, the seeds sprout, but because there is no sufficient place for the roots, they soon wither and perish.

Another portion falls on soil that has great depth, but within it are intrusive weeds. These seeds grow and take root, but soon thorns and weeds entwine around them and choke them.

The most fortunate of all these seeds are those placed in deep soil free from interference; before long, they sprout, bring forth branches and leaves, and become sturdy and fruitful.

The words of truth that emerge from the mouths of the prophets, the messengers of Allah, and their infallible successors are like these seeds. Hearts that are like hard rock will never accept them. Hearts that have a slight and weak softness accept them temporarily, then cast them out. And hearts that are ready to accept, but in which the thorns of whims, desires, lusts, and base qualities have grown, find the impact of the truth neutralized.

Only those hearts accept, nurture, and bring to fruition the words of these great leaders which are governed by a spirit of truth-seeking and are free from these negative traits—and those are the hearts of the believers. Yes: "And remind, for indeed, the reminder benefits the believers" (وَ ذَكِّرْ فَإِنَّ الذِّكْرى‌ تَنْفَعُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ).

^1: "Bal" (Rather) in the above verse is "idrabiyyah" (used for transition/rectification). ^1: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 9, p. 161.

Ayahs

وَمَا خَلَقْتُ ٱلْجِنَّ وَٱلْإِنسَ إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُونِ (٥٦)
I did not create the jinn and the humans except that they may worship Me. (56)
مَآ أُرِيدُ مِنْهُم مِّن رِّزْقٍ وَمَآ أُرِيدُ أَن يُطْعِمُونِ (٥٧)
I desire no provision from them, nor do I desire that they should feed Me. (57)
إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلرَّزَّاقُ ذُو ٱلْقُوَّةِ ٱلْمَتِينُ (٥٨)
Indeed it is Allah who is the All-provider, Powerful and All-strong. (58)

Commentary

The Purpose of Human Creation from the Perspective of the Quran

One of the most important questions every individual asks themselves is: "Why were we created?" and "What is the purpose of the creation of human beings and their coming into this world?"

The above verses answer this important and universal question with concise and profound expressions, completing the discussion mentioned in the final verse of the preceding passage regarding the admonition and reminding of the believers. This is because it is one of the most vital principles that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله must pursue; furthermore, it clarifies the meaning of "fleeing toward Allah" which appeared a few verses prior.

He says: "I did not create the jinn and mankind except that they should worship Me" (وَمَا خَلَقْتُ الْجِنَّ وَالْإِنْسَ إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُونِ).

"I do not need them," and "I do not seek from them any provision, nor do I seek that they should feed Me!" (مَا أُرِيدُ مِنْهُمْ مِنْ رِزْقٍ وَمَا أُرِيدُ أَنْ يُطْعِمُونِ).

"It is Allah who is the Provider to all His servants, the Possessor of Power, the Steadfast" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ الرَّزَّاقُ ذُو الْقُوَّةِ الْمَتِينُ).

These few verses, which are at the peak of brevity and conciseness, pull back the curtain from a reality that everyone seeks to know, and place us before a magnificent goal.

To explain: undoubtedly, every rational and wise person who performs an action has a goal in mind for it. Since Allah is the Most Knowledgeable and Wisest of all—indeed, He is beyond comparison to anyone—the question arises: why did He create man? Did He have a deficiency that was to be remedied by the creation of man?! Did He have a need that He created us to fulfill?! This is while we know His existence is perfect in every respect, infinite upon infinite, and Self-Sufficient by Essence (Ghani bi al-Dhat).

Therefore, according to the first premise, we must accept that He had a goal; and according to the second premise, we must accept that His goal in creating man is not something that returns to His Holy Essence.

Consequently, this goal must be sought outside of His Essence—a goal that returns to the creatures themselves and serves as the means for their own perfection. This is from one perspective.

From another perspective, various expressions have been used in the verses of the Quran regarding the purpose of human creation.

In one place we read: الَّذِي خَلَقَ الْمَوْتَ وَالْحَيَاةَ لِيَبْلُوَكُمْ أَيُّكُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلًا: "He is the One who created death and life to test you [as to] which of you is best in deed" (Mulk: 2). Here, the issue of testing and examining humans in terms of "goodness of deed" is introduced as a goal.

In another verse, it is stated: اللَّهُ الَّذِي خَلَقَ سَبْعَ سَمَاوَاتٍ وَمِنَ الْأَرْضِ مِثْلَهُنَّ يَتَنَزَّلُ الْأَمْرُ بَيْنَهُنَّ لِتَعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ وَأَنَّ اللَّهَ قَدْ أَحَاطَ بِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ عِلْمًا: "Allah is He who has created seven heavens and of the earth, the like of them. His command descends among them so that you may know that Allah is over all things competent and that Allah has encompassed all things in knowledge" (Talaq: 12).

And here, "knowledge and awareness of the power and knowledge of Allah" is mentioned as a goal for the creation of the heavens and the earth (and what is within them).

In another verse, we read: وَلَوْ شَاءَ رَبُّكَ لَجَعَلَ النَّاسَ أُمَّةً وَاحِدَةً ۖ وَلَا يَزَالُونَ مُخْتَلِفِينَ إِلَّا مَنْ رَحِمَ رَبُّكَ ۚ وَلِذَٰلِكَ خَلَقَهُمْ: "And if your Lord had willed, He could have made mankind one community [without any difference], but they will not cease to differ—except whom your Lord has given mercy, and for that [mercy] He created them" (Hud: 118-119). According to this verse, Divine Mercy is the primary goal of human creation.

However, the verses under discussion emphasize only the issue of servitude and worship (ubudiyyah), and with total clarity, introduce it as the ultimate goal of the creation of jinn and mankind.

A little reflection on the concept of these verses and those similar to them shows that there is no contradiction or conflict among them. In reality, some are preliminary goals, some are intermediate, and some are the ultimate goal and its result.

The primary goal is that very "servitude" mentioned in the verses under discussion. The issues of "knowledge and learning" and "testing and trial" are goals that lie on the path of servitude, and "the vast Mercy of Allah" is the result of this servitude.

In this way, it becomes clear that we were all created for the worship of the Lord. But the important thing is to know what the reality of "worship" (ibadah) is.

Is it only the performance of rituals such as bowing (ruku), prostration (sujud), standing (qiyam), sitting (qu'ud), prayer, and fasting? Or is it a reality beyond these? Although formal acts of worship are all of great importance.

To find the answer to this question, one must focus on the words "servant" (abd) and "servitude" (ubudiyyah) and analyze them.

In the Arabic language, "abd" refers to a human being who belongs entirely to his master and owner; his will is subordinate to the master's will, and his desire is subordinate to the master's desire. He owns nothing in the master's presence and never allows lethargy in obeying him.

In other words, "servitude"—as stated in linguistic texts—is the expression of the ultimate degree of humility before the Worshipped. For this reason, only He can be the Worshipped who has bestowed the ultimate favor and honor, and He is none other than Allah.

Therefore, servitude is the ultimate peak of a human's perfection and his proximity to Allah.

Servitude is the ultimate submission before His Holy Essence.

Servitude is unconditional obedience and compliance in all fields.

And finally, perfect servitude is that a human thinks of nothing but the True Worshipped—that is, Absolute Perfection—steps only in His path, and forgets everything other than Him, even himself!

And this is the ultimate goal of human creation, for the attainment of which God has provided a field of testing, given knowledge and awareness to man, and its final result is being submerged in the ocean of His "Mercy."

Key Points

1- Allah is Absolutely Self-Sufficient

The phrase "I do not seek from them any provision, nor do I seek that they should feed Me" is actually an allusion to the Lord's self-sufficiency from everyone and everything. If He invites His servants to His servitude, it is not so that He may "gain a benefit," but rather He wishes to "bestow a bounty" upon them. This is the opposite of the issue of servitude among humans, for slaves were chosen so that they might acquire income and provision for the master, or be busy in the house with service, feeding, and hosting, and in both cases, the benefit would return to the masters. This stems from human need and necessity, but all of this is meaningless regarding Allah, for not only is He independent of all, but He provides for the needs of all through His grace and generosity, and He is the Provider (Razzaq) of all.

2- He is the Possessor of "Power" and "Steadfast"

"Matin" (Steadfast) comes from the root "matn," which originally refers to the two powerful muscles located on both sides of the spine that strengthen a person's back and prepare it to endure heavy pressures. By extension, it has come to mean complete power and strength. Therefore, mentioning it after the phrase "Dhu al-Quwwah" (Possessor of Power) is for emphasis, because "Dhu al-Quwwah" refers to the principle of the Lord's power, while "Matin" refers to the perfection of His power. When it is accompanied by the word "Razzaq" (The Provider), which is also an intensive form, it establishes the truth that Allah possesses the ultimate ability and dominance in giving provision to His servants. In every corner of this vast world, in the depths of the seas, in the midst of valleys, atop mountains, in the heart of rocks, and at any point in the celestial spheres they may be, He delivers the necessary provision to them, and all are gathered at the table of His bounty. Thus, if He created them, it was not due to a need, but due to grace and kindness.

3- Why are "Jinn" mentioned first?

Despite the fact that it is clearly understood from the Quranic verses that humans are superior to the race of jinn, their name precedes "mankind" in the above verse. Apparently, this is because their creation was before the creation of Adam, as we read in Surah Al-Hijr, verse 27: وَالْجَانَّ خَلَقْنَاهُ مِنْ قَبْلُ مِنْ نَارِ السَّمُومِ: "And the jinn We created before [the creation of man] from scorching fire."

4- The Philosophy of Creation from a Philosophical Perspective

We said that there is hardly anyone who has not asked themselves or others: what was the purpose of our creation? A group is constantly being born, a group leaves the world and is silenced forever; what is the purpose of these comings and goings?

Truly, if we humans did not live on this earthly sphere, what part of the universe would be ruined? And what problem would arise? Must we know why we came and why we go? And if we wish to become aware of this meaning, do we have the power?

Following this question, a multitude of other questions surround the human mind.

Whenever this question is raised by materialists, there is apparently no answer for it, because matter and nature possess no intellect or consciousness to have a goal. For this reason, they have relieved themselves in this regard and believe in the absurdity of creation and the aimlessness of existence! And how agonizing it is for a human to consider precise goals and organized plans for the details of his life—including education, business, medical treatment, hygiene, and sports—yet consider the totality of life to be absurd and aimless?! Therefore, it is no surprise that a group of them, when thinking about these issues, becomes weary of this absurd and aimless life and resorts to suicide.

But when a God-worshipper asks this question of himself, he never faces a dead end. On one hand, he knows the Creator of this world is Wise; His creation must have had a wisdom, even if we are unaware of it. On the other hand, when he looks at every single part of his organs, he finds a purpose and philosophy for each—not only for organs such as the heart, brain, vessels, and nerves, but even organs like the nails, eyelashes, the lines on the fingertips, and the hollow of the palms and soles of the feet, each has a philosophy that is all known today.

How simplistic it is for us to grant a goal for all of these, yet consider the whole to be aimless! What a naive judgment it is for us to grant a philosophy for each of the buildings in a city, but none for the city as a whole?! Is it possible for an engineer to build a massive structure where the rooms, halls, doors, windows, pools, gardens, and decors are each built based on calculation and for a purpose, yet the totality of that massive structure pursues no goal?

These are the things that give a God-worshipping and believing person the certainty that his creation had a very grand goal, which he must strive to find with the power of intellect and knowledge.

It is strange that these proponents of the absurdity of creation, in every branch of the natural sciences they enter, look for a goal to interpret various phenomena, and they do not rest until they find that goal. They are not even willing to consider the existence of a small natural gland in a corner of the body to be idle, and they may study and experiment for years to find the philosophy of its existence; yet when they reach the origin of human creation, they explicitly say it has no goal! What a startling contradiction?! In any case, faith in the wisdom of God on one hand, and attention to the philosophies of the components of human existence on the other, makes us believers that there was a great goal for the creation of man.

Now we must search for this goal and, as far as we are able, identify it and step in its path.

Attention to several premises can create lamps and searchlights that illuminate this dark path for us:

1- We always have a goal in our actions, and this goal is usually to remove our deficiencies and needs. Even if we serve another or take the hand of a troubled person and save them from distress, or even if we practice altruism and sacrifice, those too remedy a kind of spiritual deficiency in us and fulfill our sacred needs.

And because regarding the attributes and actions of God we often fall into the trap of comparing Him to ourselves, the thought may sometimes arise: what deficiency did God have that was removed by our creation?! Or if we read in the above verses that the goal of human creation is worship, we say: what need does He have for our worship?

While these ways of thinking stem from that very comparison of the attributes of the Creator and the creature, and the Necessary and the contingent.

By virtue of our existence being limited, we strive to remove our deficiencies, and our actions are all in this path. But regarding an Infinite Existence, this meaning is not possible; we must seek the goal of His actions outside of His existence.

He is a bountiful spring and a blessing-creating source who takes creatures under His protection, nurtures them, and leads them from deficiency to perfection. This is the real goal of our servitude and worship, and this is the philosophy of our acts of worship and supplications, which are all educational classes for our perfection.

In this way, we conclude that the goal of our creation is the progress and perfection of our being.

Fundamentally, the principle of creation is a massive evolutionary step—that is, bringing something from non-existence into existence, making "is" from "is not," and bringing it from the stage of zero to the stage of a number.

After this massive evolutionary step, other stages of perfection begin, and all religious and divine programs are in this very path.

2- Here a question arises: if the goal of creation is bounty upon the servants, not benefit for the Creator, and this bounty is through the perfection of humans, why did this Generous and Noble God not create the servants perfect from the beginning, so that they might all take their place in the proximity of His presence and benefit from the blessings of closeness to His Holy Essence?

The answer to this question is clear: human perfection is not something that can be created by "compulsion"; rather, it is a long and lengthy path that humans must traverse with their own feet, and they must lay its design with their own will, decision, and voluntary actions.

If a large sum of money is taken from someone by force and at the point of a bayonet to build a hospital, does this action have any moral effect or spiritual perfection for him?

Certainly not. But if he contributes even a single rial to such a sacred goal by his own will and desire, he has traversed the path of moral perfection to that same extent.

From this statement, we conclude that God must identify this path for us through commands, obligations, and educational programs conveyed by His prophets and the power of intellect, and we must traverse this path with our own choice and will.

3- Again, another question is raised here. When some hear the above explanations, they say: "Very well, the goal of our creation was human perfection, or in other words, proximity to the Lord and the movement of a deficient existence toward an infinitely perfect existence; but what is the goal of this perfection?"

The answer to this question is also clarified by the phrase that perfection is the ultimate goal, or in other words, the "Goal of Goals" (Ghayat al-Ghayat).

To explain: if we ask a student, "Why are you studying?" He says: "To enter university." Again, if we ask, "Why do you want university?" He says: "To become, for example, a capable doctor or engineer." We say: "Why do you want a doctorate or engineering degree?" He says: "To perform positive activity and also have a good income." Again we say: "Why do you want a good income?" He says: "To have a dignified and comfortable life." Finally, we ask, "Why do you want a comfortable and dignified life?" Here we see the tone of his speech changes, and he says: "Well, to have a comfortable and dignified life," meaning he repeats the previous answer.

This is proof that he has reached the final answer, the so-called "Goal of Goals" of his work, beyond which there is no other answer, and it constitutes the ultimate goal. This is in matters of material life.

In spiritual life, the matter is the same. When it is asked, "What is the purpose of the coming of the prophets, the revelation of the heavenly books, and the obligations and educational programs?" We say: "For human perfection and proximity to God." And if they ask, "For what purpose is perfection and proximity to the Lord?" We say, "For proximity to the Lord!" Meaning this is the ultimate goal. In other words, we want everything for perfection and proximity to God, but we want proximity to God for itself (meaning for proximity to the Lord).

4- Once more, a question is raised here regarding a tradition (hadith) which states that God says: "I was a hidden treasure, so I loved to be known, and I created the creation so that I might be known" (Kuntu kanzan makhfiyyan fa-ahbabtu an u'rafa fa-khalaqtu al-khalqa li-kay u'rafa). How does this tradition harmonize with what you have said?

In response, we say: besides the fact that this tradition is a solitary report (khabar al-wahid) and a solitary report is not sufficient in matters of creed, the concept of the tradition is that the knowledge of God for the creation is a means for their perfection. That is, "I loved that the bounty of My mercy should encompass everywhere; for this reason, I created the creation, and for their journey toward perfection, I taught them the way and manner of My gnosis (ma'rifah), because gnosis and knowledge of Me is the secret of their perfection."

Yes, the servants must know the Essence of God, who is the source of all perfections, align themselves with His perfections, and provide a reflection of them within their own existence, so that a spark of those attributes of perfection and majesty shines in their being. For perfection and proximity to God is not possible except through adopting His character (takhalluq bi-akhlaqih), and this adoption is secondary to knowledge (reflect on this).

5- With attention to what we have said in the sections above, we approach the final conclusion and say: worship and servitude to God means stepping in the path of His will, surrendering the soul and spirit to Him, placing His love in the heart, and adorning oneself with His character.

And if in the above verses "worship" is introduced as the ultimate goal of creation, its meaning is this very thing which is referred to in other terms as "human perfection."

Yes, the perfect human is the same as the true servant of God!

5- A Look at Islamic Narrations Regarding the Philosophy of Human Creation

Above, we pursued the issue of the goal of human creation through two paths: one through the interpretation of Quranic verses, and the other through the philosophical path, and both led us to the same point.

Now it is time to follow this fateful issue through the third path, namely through Islamic narrations.

Reflection on the following narrations, which are a part of these traditions, gives us a deeper insight into this matter:

In a tradition from Imam Musa ibn Ja'far عليه السلام, it is narrated that he was asked about the meaning of the Prophet's statement: "Act, for everyone is facilitated for that for which he was created" (I'malu fa-kullun muyassarun lima khuliqa lah). The Imam said:

"Indeed, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, created the jinn and mankind to worship Him, and He did not create them to disobey Him, and that is His saying, the Mighty and Majestic: 'And I did not create the jinn and mankind except that they should worship Me.' So He facilitated each for that for which he was created. So woe to him who prefers blindness over guidance." [^1]

This tradition is a meaningful allusion to the truth that since God created humans for an evolutionary goal, He has provided the means for it in terms of both creation (takwin) and legislation (tashri') and placed them at their disposal.

In another tradition from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read that Imam al-Husayn عليه السلام came before his companions and said:

"Indeed, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, did not create the servants except so that they might know Him. When they know Him, they worship Him, and when they worship Him, they become independent through His worship from the worship of anyone other than Him." [^1]

6- Answer to a Question

Another question raised here is: if God created the servants for servitude, then why does a group take the path of disbelief? Is it possible for God's will to deviate from His goal?

Those who raise this objection have, in reality, confused the creative will (iradah takwiniyyah) with the legislative will (iradah tashri'iyyah). This is because the goal was not compulsory worship; rather, worship and servitude are coupled with will and choice. In such a context, the goal manifests in the form of preparing the grounds. For example, when it is said, "I have built this mosque for people to pray in," its meaning is that I have made it ready for this work, not that I force people to pray. Likewise in other cases, such as building a school for education, a hospital for treatment, and a library for study.

In this way, God has made this human ready for obedience and servitude and has provided every kind of means, including intellect, emotions, and various powers from within, and prophets, heavenly books, and legislative programs from without.

It is certain that this meaning is the same for the believer and the disbeliever, although the believer has utilized his potential and the disbeliever has not.

Therefore, in a tradition from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read that when he was asked about the interpretation of this verse (وَمَا خَلَقْتُ الْجِنَّ وَالْإِنْسَ إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُونِ), he said:

"He created them for worship."

The narrator says: I asked: "Specifically or generally?" (khassatan am 'ammatan)

The Imam said: "Generally" ('ammatan). [^1] And in another tradition from the same Imam عليه السلام, it is narrated that when he was asked about the interpretation of this verse, he said:

"He created them to command them to worship." [^2]

This is an allusion to the fact that the goal was not compulsion toward worship and servitude, but rather preparing the ground for it, and this is true regarding all people. [^3]

[^1]: Tawhid al-Saduq (as cited in Al-Mizan, vol. 18, p. 423). [^1]: 'Ilal al-Shara'i' by al-Saduq (according to the same source). [^1]: Bihar al-Anwar, vol. 5, p. 314, hadith 7. [^2]: Same source, hadith 5. [^3]: From what has been said above, it becomes clear that the "Alif and Lam" in al-Ins and al-Jinn are for exhaustion (istighraq) and include all individuals, not for the genus in a way that would only include a specific group, as has appeared in some commentaries.

Ayahs

فَإِنَّ لِلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ ذَنُوبًا مِّثْلَ ذَنُوبِ أَصْحَـٰبِهِمْ فَلَا يَسْتَعْجِلُونِ (٥٩)
Indeed the lot of those who do wrong [now] will be like the lot of their [earlier] counterparts. So let them not ask Me to hasten on [that fate]. (59)
فَوَيْلٌ لِّلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِن يَوْمِهِمُ ٱلَّذِى يُوعَدُونَ (٦٠)
Woe to the faithless for the day they are promised! (60)

Commentary

These Too Share in the Divine Punishment

The two aforementioned verses, which are the final verses of Surah al-Dhariyat, are in fact a kind of conclusion drawn from the various verses of this Surah—especially those verses that spoke about the fate of previous nations such as the people of Pharaoh, Lot, ‘Ad, and Thamud, as well as the preceding verses that discussed the purpose of creation.

It says: "Now that it has become clear that this polytheistic and sinful nation has deviated from the primary purpose of creation, they must know that they too have a large share of Divine punishment, just like the share their counterparts among the previous nations had" (فَإِنَّ لِلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا ذَنُوباً مِثْلَ ذَنُوبِ أَصْحابِهِمْ‌).

"Therefore, let them not be in a hurry" and repeatedly ask: if the Divine punishment is true, why does it not come to us? (فَلا يَسْتَعْجِلُونِ‌) [1]

[1]: It should be noted that the nun in "yastajilun" carries a kasrah, whereas the nun of a plural verb should carry a fathah; the reason is that it was originally "yastajiluni" (hurry Me).

The use of the term "oppression" (dhulm) regarding this group is because "polytheism" (shirk) and disbelief are the greatest forms of oppression. This is because the essence of oppression is to place something in a position other than its rightful place, and certainly, placing an idol in the place of God is considered the most significant instance of oppression. For this reason, they deserve the same fate that the previous polytheistic nations faced.

"Dhanub" (on the pattern of qabul) originally means a horse with a long tail, and also large buckets that have a trailing end.

In the past, to draw water from a well using animals, they would prepare massive buckets that had a trailing part; in addition to the bucket's mouth, a rope was attached to its tail which was used to empty that massive bucket.

Since these buckets were sometimes used to divide water among several groups, giving each group one or more buckets, this word also came to be used to mean "a share" or "portion." In the verse under discussion, it is used in this sense, though it refers to a large share. Lana dhanubun wa lakum dhanubun / Fa-in abaytum fa-lana al-qalibu! [1]

Is the intention in this verse a threat of punishment in this world, or the punishment of the Hereafter? A group of commentators has accepted the second meaning, while some have suggested the possibility of the first.

In our view, the evidence points toward the punishment of this world. Firstly, the haste shown by some of the disbelievers was mostly because they would say to the Prophet: "If you are truthful, then why does the Divine punishment not descend upon us?" This certainly refers to the punishment of this world. [2]

[1]: One of the Arab poets says: "A large bucket for us and a large bucket for you—and if you refuse, then the entire well is ours!" [2]: Refer to Surah al-An'am verses 57 and 58, Surah al-Naml verse 72, and similar verses. Of course, this expression in Quranic verses is occasionally used regarding the Resurrection as well.

Secondly, the expression "like the portion of their companions" (مِثْلَ ذَنُوبِ أَصْحابِهِمْ‌) apparently refers to the fate of the nations mentioned in this Surah, such as the people of Lot, Pharaoh, ‘Ad, and Thamud, each of whom was afflicted by a type of worldly punishment and perished.

Here, a question arises: if the verse pertains to worldly punishment, why was this Divine promise not fulfilled regarding them?

There are two answers to this question:

  1. This promise was fulfilled regarding many of them, such as Abu Jahl and a group of others, during the Battle of Badr and other instances.

  2. The descent of this punishment for all of them was conditional upon their not returning to Allah and not repenting from polytheism. When the majority of them embraced faith during the Conquest of Mecca, this condition was nullified and the Divine punishment was averted.

In the final verse, He completes the threat of worldly punishment with the threat of the punishment of the Hereafter, saying: "Woe to those who disbelieve, from their Day which they are promised" (فَوَيْلٌ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ يَوْمِهِمُ الَّذِي يُوعَدُونَ‌). Just as this Surah began with the issue of the Return (Ma'ad) and the Resurrection, it ends with an emphasis on the same issue. [1]

[1]: Some have suggested that this verse also refers to worldly punishment, whereas such expressions in the Holy Quran are usually used for the Day of Judgment.

"Wayl" in the Arabic language is used in cases where an individual or individuals fall into destruction; it conveys the meaning of punishment and wretchedness, and according to some, it has a more intense connotation than "punishment" ('adhab).

The words "Wayl," "Ways," and "Wayh" are used in Arabic when someone feels regret for another's state; however, "Wayl" is used for ugly and vile acts, "Ways" is used in a context of belittlement, and "Wayh" is used in a context of pity.

A group has said: "Wayl" is a well or a valley in Hell, but the intention of these speakers is not that it carries this meaning linguistically, but rather it is the description of a specific instance.

This expression is used in the Holy Quran in many instances, including regarding the disbelievers, polytheists, liars, deniers, sinners, those who give short measure, and the heedless performers of prayer. However, its most frequent use in the Holy Quran is regarding the deniers; for instance, in Surah al-Mursalat, this sentence is repeated ten times:

"Woe on that Day to the deniers" (وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلْمُكَذِّبِينَ‌): "Woe on the Day of Resurrection for those who denied the Prophets and the Divine signs."

O Allah! Protect us through Your grace from the punishment of that Great Day and its terrifying disgrace. O God! Grant us the readiness to accept, the success of servitude, and the honor of being Your servants.

O Lord! Do not afflict us with the painful fate of the nations who denied Your Prophets and signs or cast them behind their backs, and awaken us from the sleep of heedlessness before the opportunity is lost. Amen, O Lord of the Worlds.

End of Surah al-Dhariyat, Friday, 10th of Safar, 1406 AH, corresponding to 3rd of Aban, 1364 SH.
